gaps.feature: Paste selected item into the previously focused app
gaps.description: The standalone app's primary, most-used action: Enter (or double-click / menu) restores focus to the prior app, sets the clipboard, and synthesizes Ctrl+V (with verification/retry, per-app override hotkeys, Alt+V for Claude CLI, and direct UIA paste into Explorer search). In the palette there is NO paste action whatsoever — Enter opens a preview page, and the inline/context action set is limited to copy/open/reveal/pin/unpin (ClipHistoryActionCommand only handles copy/open/reveal in-process). A clipboard manager whose flagship gesture (paste-on-Enter) is absent is fundamentally less useful than the standalone app. The docs themselves flag paste as conditional/WPF-only pending reliable focus handoff.
gaps.paletteApproach: Add a 'Paste' command as the DEFAULT command (primary Invoke) on each ListItem instead of the preview page, so Enter pastes. Implement a ClipPasteCommand that: (1) calls ClipClipboardWriter.SetItem (reuse the existing in-process WinRT writer) honoring ClipboardPasteData.Create with PasteFormatPreference; (2) returns CommandResult.Dismiss() to close the palette and let the OS restore focus to the prior foreground window; (3) synthesizes Ctrl+V via SendInput/keybd_event after a short focus-settle delay. Reuse Clip.Core ClipboardPasteData + Clip.Shell's paste/focus logic (PasteSelected, ResolveOverrideHotkey, AutoAltVForClaudeCli, TryPasteDirectlyIntoExplorerSearch) extracted into a shared helper. If reliable focus handoff inside Command Palette is not achievable, fall back to invoking the watcher's 'paste <id>' verb (Clip.Watcher PasteLatestFromTray path) as the action Executable.
gaps.executableCodeRef: MainWindow.xaml.cs:3841 PasteSelected; 3852-3876 ResolveOverrideHotkey/AutoAltVForClaudeCli/TryPasteDirectlyIntoExplorerSearch; Clip.Watcher/Program.cs:184-186 paste verb; ClipboardPasteData.cs:20 Create

gaps.feature: Paste format preference applied at paste time (original formatting vs plain)
gaps.description: Clip.Core supports building a paste payload with HTML/RTF when PasteFormatPreference.OriginalFormatting is set, and the standalone app honors DefaultPasteFormat on every paste. The palette never pastes, and its copy path hard-codes PlainText (ClipClipboardWriter.SetItem calls ClipboardPasteData.Create(item, PasteFormatPreference.PlainText)), so even copying drops original formatting regardless of the user's setting. 'Formatted' is shown as a tag but the formatting is never actually delivered.
gaps.paletteApproach: Read DefaultPasteFormat from ClipSharedSettings (extend the shared settings snapshot to include PasteFormatPreference) and pass it into ClipboardPasteData.Create for both the new Paste command and the existing copy writer. When HasOriginalFormatting(item) is true, populate the WinRT DataPackage with SetHtmlFormat/SetRtf in addition to SetText. Optionally expose a secondary 'Paste as plain text' context command for per-invocation override (mirrors standalone behavior).
gaps.executableCodeRef: MainWindow.xaml.cs:3845-3848,3888-3898 ClipboardItemForPasteFormat; ClipboardPasteData.cs:13,20 HasOriginalFormatting/Create; ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:174 (hard-coded PlainText)

gaps.description: Standalone offers 'Append to Clipboard' for Text/Link items to accumulate content onto the current clipboard text. No equivalent exists in the palette action set (not produced by ClipboardHistoryListCommand.ForHistoryItem and not handled by any palette command).
gaps.paletteApproach: Add an 'append' action id in Clip.Core ClipboardHistoryListAction.ForHistoryItem for Text/Link items, then handle it in-process in ClipHistoryActionCommand: read current clipboard text via WinRT Clipboard.GetContent, concatenate the item's text, and SetContent the combined value. Reuse Clip.Shell AppendText logic.
gaps.executableCodeRef: MainWindow.xaml.cs:4827 AppendText; menu 3170; Clip.Watcher/Program.cs:202-204 append verb

gaps.description: Standalone has a Share submenu invoking the Windows DataTransferManager share sheet and an optional Blip share target. Clip.Core already provides ClipboardSharePayload.Create and BlipShareLaunchPlan, but the palette exposes no Share action at all.
gaps.paletteApproach: Add a 'Share' context command that builds a ClipboardSharePayload.Create(item) and invokes the Windows share UI. In a packaged Command Palette extension use Windows.ApplicationModel.DataTransfer.DataTransferManager (requires an HWND/interop init) or fall back to launching the watcher/shell share path. For Blip, gate a 'Blip' command on BlipShareLaunchPlan.IsInstalled() and Process.Start its --file argument list. Reuse ClipboardSharePayload cleanup.
gaps.executableCodeRef: MainWindow.xaml.cs:5253 ShareItem; ClipboardSharePayload.cs:19 Create; BlipShareLaunchPlan.cs:16,37 IsInstalled/Create

gaps.description: Standalone offers an Open With app picker (default/recommended/recent-app prioritization, multi-source discovery, packaged-app launch) for image/file items. The palette has no Open With action; docs mark it WPF-only. A Command Palette is an ideal host for exactly this searchable picker, so its absence is a real native-experience gap.
gaps.paletteApproach: Add an 'Open With' context command that pushes a secondary DynamicListPage listing discovered apps (icon/name/source) with native search, where selecting an app calls ClipboardItemLaunchCommand.CreateOpenStartInfo(item, appPath). Extract Clip's app-discovery (file associations, Start Menu, registered app paths, recent choices, packaged apps) from the standalone into a shared Clip.Core service so both surfaces reuse it; persist recent-per-extension choices.

gaps.feature: Time-bucket grouping (Today / Yesterday / This week / This month / This year / Older)
gaps.description: Standalone groups history into time-bucket section headers below Pinned, making large histories scannable. The palette only emits two sections, 'Pinned Items' and 'Recent Items' (ClipHistorypage CreateItems), with no temporal grouping, so a long unpinned list is one flat block.
gaps.paletteApproach: Compute a DateKey per item (reuse Clip.Shell DateKey logic, ideally promoted into Clip.Core alongside ClipboardHistoryListItem) and set each ListItem.Section to the bucket name ('Today', 'Yesterday', etc.) instead of a single 'Recent Items'. Keep Pinned items in their own leading section. Order sections by recency.

gaps.description: Standalone has a date dropdown attached to the All pill to filter by recency. The palette's only dropdown is the kind/pinned filter (7 entries); there is no date filtering.
gaps.paletteApproach: Add a second IFilters dropdown (or extend ClipHistoryFilters with a date dimension) whose selection sets a date predicate applied in CreateItems (reuse DateKey). Because Command Palette currently surfaces one Filters dropdown, either combine kind+date into a richer filter list or apply date as an additional in-memory filter keyed off SearchText conventions.

gaps.description: Standalone's File pill has a dropdown that filters Files items by sub-kind, dynamically built from kinds present in history. The palette has only a single flat 'Files' filter with no sub-kind narrowing.
gaps.paletteApproach: Extend ClipHistoryFilters with file sub-kind entries (reuse Clip.Core FileCategoryKey/LabelForFileKey) shown when the Files filter is active, and apply the sub-kind predicate in CreateItems. Build the list dynamically from categories present in the current result set.

gaps.feature: Rich file previews: HTML (WebView2), PDF first page, Office/Visio first page, text files
gaps.description: Standalone renders live previews for HTML (WebView2), PDF first page, Word/Excel/PowerPoint/Visio first page, image files, and truncated text files, with loading/placeholder states. The palette's preview is limited to inline image markdown for Image-kind items and plain text/file-path text; for a Files item pointing at a PDF/Office/HTML doc it shows only the path string, not a rendered preview. This is a large fidelity gap for file-heavy histories.
gaps.paletteApproach: For text-type files, read via Clip.Core TextFilePreviewReader and embed as fenced Markdown in the details Body/preview page (cheap, already available). For PDF/Office/Visio, render a first-page thumbnail to a temp PNG using the existing WatcherPdfPreviewRenderer / WatcherStaticDocumentPreviewRenderer (move them into a shared lib) and embed via the same file:// cmdpal image markdown used for images. WebView2 live HTML is not feasible inside Command Palette; substitute a rendered/static snapshot or a text excerpt. Do this lazily off the list-render path to preserve cold-open performance.

gaps.feature: Original-formatting / RTF / HTML text preview fidelity
gaps.description: Standalone shows text in a monospaced wrapping preview and stores HtmlText/RtfText. The palette text preview is plain monospace markdown capped at 30 lines / 2000-12000 chars and never renders rich formatting; acceptable for a palette but lower fidelity than standalone. Listed as partial for completeness.
gaps.paletteApproach: Keep plain-text preview (Command Palette markdown cannot host RTF/WebView2). Optionally surface a 'Formatted' indicator (already a tag) and ensure full text hydration via store.GetItem on the preview page (already done) so long text isn't truncated below the standalone's limit.

gaps.feature: Expand image to full overlay with pan/zoom
gaps.description: Standalone can expand an image into a full-window overlay with mouse-wheel zoom and drag-pan. The palette shows a fixed-size inline image (cmdpal maxwidth/maxheight hints) with no expand/zoom/pan interaction.
gaps.paletteApproach: Command Palette has no custom canvas for pan/zoom; closest native approximation is offering an 'Open' action (already present) to view the image in the OS viewer, plus rendering the preview-page image at the larger 900x520 hint (already done). Document as a deliberate platform limitation rather than attempting an in-palette zoom surface.

gaps.feature: Live history updates while the palette is open
gaps.description: Docs intend live updates while open; standalone updates as you copy. The palette only refreshes when GetItems re-runs and its cache invalidates on index/history file mtime change, but there is no push/watch — newly copied items won't appear until the user retypes/re-filters or the list is otherwise re-queried. So it is not truly live.
gaps.paletteApproach: Add a FileSystemWatcher on the history/top-index files (or a lightweight polling timer) inside ClipHistoryPage that calls InvalidateItems()/RaiseItemsChanged() when the store changes, so new captures appear without user interaction. Reuse the existing CurrentIndexStampUtc check to avoid redundant reloads.
gaps.executableCodeRef: docs/cloud-design-brief.md:22 (live updates); ClipHistoryPage.cs:80-94,110-114 (mtime cache, manual invalidation only)

gaps.feature: Duplicate Information rendering on details (FactSet + markdown table)
gaps.description: The details pane builds the metadata both as a native FactSet (Metadata) AND as a duplicate '## Information' markdown table in the Body, so the same fields render twice. This is a polish/native-feel regression versus the standalone's single frozen Information panel.
gaps.paletteApproach: Drop the markdown '## Information' table from DetailsBody and rely solely on the native Metadata FactSet; keep only the '## Preview' section in the Body. Mirror standalone's single Information panel.

gaps.feature: Full in-palette Settings parity (history limit, max item size, clipboard folder, theme, paste format, startup, privacy excludes, per-app overrides, hotkey rebinding, reset)
gaps.description: The native ClipSettingsPage exposes only 3 controls (open-mode, tray icon, update-check). Standalone settings cover history limit, max item size, clipboard data folder (open/change/reset), theme + app icon, default paste format, run-at-startup, privacy excluded apps, per-app hotkey overrides, hotkey rebinding/reset, and reset-all. Docs explicitly mark most of these WPF-only, but for the palette to feel as full-featured the user shouldn't have to leave to Clip.exe to change basic behavior.
gaps.paletteApproach: Extend ClipSharedSettings and ClipSettingsPage with the settings that don't require the WPF capture engine: history limit, max item size, default paste format, run-at-startup (write the Run key), and clipboard data folder (call ClipboardHistoryStore.SetContentRootPath). These are all backed by Clip.Core (ClipStoragePaths, SetContentRootPath, ApplyHistoryLimit). Privacy excludes, per-app hotkey overrides, and the capture hotkey legitimately belong to the watcher/WPF host — surface them read-only or via 'Open Clip.exe settings'. Theme is host-controlled in Command Palette; leave as WPF-only.
gaps.executableCodeRef: MainWindow.xaml.cs:10383 HistoryLimitRow / 10406 MaxItemSizeRow / 10500 DataFolderRow / 10660 DefaultPasteFormatRow / 10332 StartupRow; ClipSettingsPage.cs:19-85 (only 3 controls); ClipboardHistoryStore.cs:1037 ApplyHistoryLimit, 1575 SetContentRootPath

gaps.description: Standalone and tray can write a debug log snapshot (Ctrl+Shift+L) and the About page can open/save logs. The palette has no diagnostics command (it only delegates check-updates/settings to Clip.exe).
gaps.paletteApproach: Add a page-level context command 'Save log snapshot' that either calls a shared Clip.Core diagnostics writer or delegates to Clip.exe via --tray-action=save-log (same delegation pattern already used for check-updates/settings in ClipShellActionCommand).

gaps.feature: Windows clipboard-history import trigger from the palette
gaps.description: The watcher auto-imports Windows clipboard history and Clip.Command exposes import-windows-history. The palette reads resulting history but offers no way to trigger an import on demand; docs mark it watcher/helper-only.
gaps.paletteApproach: Add an optional page-level command 'Import Windows clipboard history' that runs ClipboardHistoryImportService against a WindowsClipboardHistorySource in-process, or delegates to Clip.Command.exe import-windows-history via ClipExecutableLocator, then InvalidateItems() to refresh the list. Reuse Clip.Core ClipboardHistoryImportService.

gaps.feature: More than 25 items / paging / configurable list limit
gaps.description: The palette hard-codes Limit=25 (ClipHistoryPage.Limit), while standalone shows the full history (history limit up to Unlimited, default 500) with time-bucket scrolling. Users with large pinned+recent sets cannot reach older items in the palette even via search-narrowing beyond the 25 cap returned by ClipboardHistoryListCommand.Create.
gaps.paletteApproach: Raise the default limit (ClipboardHistoryListCommand caps at 100) and/or make it configurable from settings; consider incremental loading by increasing the limit when the user scrolls/searches. Since search is performed natively in Clip.Core against the index, a larger limit is cheap. Respect the user's HistoryLimit setting as the ceiling.

gaps.feature: Inline action coverage on preview card (only copy/pin/unpin/open/reveal, max 4)
gaps.description: The Adaptive Card preview limits inline buttons to copy/pin/unpin/open/reveal and Take(4), so rename, edit-text, delete, save-as-file, copy-path, append, share, and (missing) paste are not reachable as inline buttons on the preview card — only via the list row's MoreCommands context menu. The preview page therefore can't perform the full action set the standalone preview/header offers.
gaps.paletteApproach: Either expose the full action list as the preview page's Commands/MoreCommands (ClipItemPreviewPage already sets per-item actions) and/or raise the inline-button whitelist to include paste/delete/rename. Keep destructive actions (delete) visually distinct. Ensure Enter on the preview page maps to the default (Paste) action.
gaps.executableCodeRef: ClipItemPreviewCard.cs:208-225 BuildActions (whitelist + Take(4)); MainWindow.xaml.cs:3215 ShowStyledMenu (full standalone action set)

gaps.feature: Tray: paste-latest / quick paste of most recent item without opening UI
gaps.description: Standalone/watcher offer 'Paste latest item' from the tray and a tray-action automation path to paste the newest item without showing any UI. There is no palette-side equivalent quick command to paste the latest item.
gaps.paletteApproach: Add a top-level or page-level command 'Paste latest item' that fetches store.QueryRecentItemSummaries(1) and runs the new Paste command on it (Dismiss + Ctrl+V), or delegates to the watcher's paste-latest path. Low priority since the palette is itself the quick-access surface.

blueprints.feature: Paste selected item into the previously focused app (Enter = paste) in the Clip Command Palette extension
blueprints.sdkNotes:
Default command = primary Invoke. In Microsoft.CommandPalette.Extensions, a ListItem's `Command` property is the Enter action; `MoreCommands` are the Ctrl+K context menu. Today ClipHistoryPage.CreateListItem (ClipHistoryPage.cs:190) sets `new ListItem(new ClipItemPreviewPage(...))`, so Enter opens the preview ContentPage. To make Enter paste, set `Command = new ClipPasteCommand(...)` (an InvokableCommand) and DEMOTE preview to a `CommandContextItem` at the front of MoreCommands (give it a clear name like "Preview"). Build ClipPasteCommand as `internal sealed partial class ClipPasteCommand : InvokableCommand` exactly like ClipHistoryActionCommand (ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:11), with Name="Paste", Icon=new IconInfo("") (or ""). Invoke() returns ICommandResult — use CommandResult.Dismiss() to close the palette so the OS restores foreground to the prior app; use CommandResult.KeepOpen() on failure and surface errors via ToastStatusMessage/StatusMessage (already used in ClipHistoryActionCommand.ShowError, ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:136). Errors/toasts: reuse MessageState.Error pattern.

CRITICAL SDK/architecture limit — why this is 'partial': the extension is an out-of-process COM server activated by the Microsoft.CommandPalette host; the extension process NEVER holds foreground focus, so it cannot capture `_returnFocusHwnd` the way Clip.Shell's WPF window does (MainWindow CaptureReturnFocus runs on the shell's own activation, MainWindow.xaml.cs:3920). The full standalone paste pipeline (RestoreReturnFocus 3944, SendPasteKeys/SendInput 4126/4162, ResolveOverrideHotkey 4205, AutoAltVForClaudeCli 4226, TryPasteDirectlyIntoExplorerSearch 3976, VerifyPasteOrRetry 4028) is tightly bound to that captured-focus state and the WPF window — it is NOT cheaply extractable. Therefore the RELIABLE primary implementation is to delegate to the existing watcher 'paste <id>' verb (Clip.Watcher/Program.cs:184 -> TrySetClipboardItem 253 -> SendKeys.SendWait("^v") 279), which runs in a normal foreground-eligible Win32 process after the palette dismisses. ClipExecutableLocator.Resolve already locates helper exes (ClipExecutableLocator.cs:13); note ForHistoryItem emits Executable "Clip.Watcher.exe" for copy and "Clip.Command.exe" for open/reveal (ClipboardHistoryListCommand.cs:180,265) so resolve "Clip.Watcher.exe" for the paste verb. In-process clipboard set (ClipClipboardWriter.SetItem, ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:165) can still be used to pre-stage the clipboard honoring ClipboardPasteData.Create/PasteFormatPreference (ClipboardPasteData.cs:20), but the keystroke synthesis must happen from the helper process, not the extension host.
implementationSteps: Add a 'paste' action to the action model so the palette has a first-class paste verb and DefaultActionId points at it. In ClipboardHistoryListAction.ForHistoryItem (ClipboardHistoryListCommand.cs:171) insert, as the FIRST action when CanCopy(item) is true (ClipboardHistoryListCommand.cs:283), a new ClipboardHistoryListAction(Id:"paste", Label:"Paste", Executable:"Clip.Watcher.exe", Arguments:["paste", item.Id], RequiresFullItem:true). Because DefaultActionId is set to actions.FirstOrDefault()?.Id (ClipboardHistoryListCommand.cs:156), paste becomes the default for pasteable items; copy stays as the next action.

Create ClipPasteCommand.cs (new file) modeled on ClipHistoryActionCommand (ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:11): internal sealed partial class ClipPasteCommand : InvokableCommand. Ctor takes (ClipboardHistoryListItem item, ClipboardHistoryStore store); set Name="Paste", Icon=new IconInfo(""). In Invoke(): (a) resolve the full item via store.GetItem(item.Id); if null -> ShowError + CommandResult.KeepOpen(); (b) pre-stage the clipboard in-process via ClipClipboardWriter.SetItem(fullItem) (ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:165) so format preference (ClipboardPasteData.Create, ClipboardPasteData.cs:20) is honored even before the helper runs; (c) locate Clip.Watcher.exe via ClipExecutableLocator.Resolve("Clip.Watcher.exe") (ClipExecutableLocator.cs:13); (d) if found, Process.Start a ProcessStartInfo with ArgumentList {"paste", item.Id} (same shape as ClipHistoryActionCommand.Invoke fallback, ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:42-54) then return CommandResult.Dismiss() so the palette closes and the OS returns foreground to the prior app, where the watcher process synthesizes Ctrl+V; (e) if the watcher exe is NOT found, fall back to copy-only: clipboard is already staged in step (b), show an informational ToastStatusMessage('Copied — press Ctrl+V to paste') and return CommandResult.Dismiss().

Harden the watcher 'paste' verb for the no-prior-focus race. In Clip.Watcher/Program.cs SetClipboard/TrySetClipboardItem (Program.cs:245,253) the current flow is SetText/SetImage/SetFileDropList then SendKeys.SendWait("^v") (Program.cs:279). Add a short focus-settle delay before the SendKeys call (e.g. Thread.Sleep(~80-120ms) or a brief GetForegroundWindow poll) so the palette has fully dismissed and the OS has restored foreground before the keystroke; gate it behind the existing paste:true path so 'copy' is unaffected. (Optional but recommended for parity: accept an extra arg so the verb can skip re-setting the clipboard when the palette already staged it, avoiding a double clipboard write.)

Rewire ClipHistoryPage.CreateListItem (ClipHistoryPage.cs:188-210) so Enter pastes. Change `new ListItem(new ClipItemPreviewPage(...))` to `new ListItem(BuildDefaultCommand(item, store))` where BuildDefaultCommand returns `new ClipPasteCommand(item, store)` when item.DefaultActionId == "paste" (i.e., pasteable), else falls back to the existing ClipItemPreviewPage for non-pasteable kinds. In CreateContextCommand (ClipHistoryPage.cs:215) route action.Id == "paste" -> new ClipPasteCommand(item, store), and ADD a 'Preview' CommandContextItem (wrapping ClipItemPreviewPage) to the front of MoreCommands so users can still open the preview page via Ctrl+K. Keep copy/open/reveal/pin/unpin context commands as-is.

Mirror the same default-command + Preview-context wiring in ClipItemPreviewPage.CreateContextCommand (ClipItemPreviewPage.cs:38) so when a user is on the preview page the paste action is also available, and add the 'paste' branch there too (ClipItemPreviewPage.cs:40 switch).

Build the Clip.CommandPalette project (it references Clip.Core; confirm it can also reference the Watcher exe path at runtime via ClipExecutableLocator — no compile-time dependency needed since paste is launched by process). Verify ClipPasteCommand compiles against Microsoft.CommandPalette.Extensions.Toolkit (InvokableCommand, IconInfo, CommandResult, ToastStatusMessage/StatusMessage all already used in ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs).

Manual verification: (1) focus Notepad, open Command Palette, search Clip history, press Enter on a text item -> palette dismisses, Ctrl+V fires into Notepad. (2) Image item -> bitmap pastes (Program.cs:265 SetImage path). (3) Files item -> file drop paste (Program.cs:270). (4) Watcher exe absent -> graceful copy-only toast, no crash. (5) Ctrl+K still shows Preview + copy/open/reveal/pin/unpin. (6) Confirm format preference (PlainText vs OriginalFormatting) is honored via the staged ClipClipboardWriter.SetItem path.
blueprints.responsivenessNotes: Keep Invoke() non-blocking: do the in-process ClipClipboardWriter.SetItem (fast WinRT DataPackage set, ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:165-201) synchronously, then fire-and-forget Process.Start for the watcher 'paste' verb and immediately return CommandResult.Dismiss() — do NOT wait on the child process or the OS focus restore on the extension thread (the host marshals Invoke over COM; blocking it stalls the palette UI). Push the focus-settle Thread.Sleep into the watcher process (step 3), never into the extension. Reuse the warm watcher: the watcher is already kept running (EnsureStandaloneShellWarm / tray, Program.cs:1321), but the 'paste <id>' verb spawns a fresh short-lived Clip.Watcher.exe instance via RunCommand (Program.cs:169) — that is fine and avoids IPC complexity, though a future optimization is to signal the resident watcher (it already has show-palette EventWaitHandle plumbing, Program.cs:548-569) to paste latest/by-id without a new process. Use Fluent Segoe MDL2 glyph icons (consistent with existing IconFor/IconForAction maps, ClipHistoryPage.cs:395,405) so the Paste action renders natively with no asset load. Cache nothing extra; the existing GetItems cache (ClipHistoryPage.cs:73) is unaffected since the command swap is per-item construction.

blueprints.feature: Paste format preference (original formatting vs plain) applied at paste/copy time in Command Palette
blueprints.sdkNotes: Uses WinRT Windows.ApplicationModel.DataTransfer.DataPackage (already referenced in ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:5). For original formatting: in addition to data.SetText(payload.Text), call data.SetHtmlFormat(payload.Html) when payload.Html != null and data.SetRtf(payload.Rtf) when payload.Rtf != null. SetHtmlFormat expects a full CF_HTML clipboard fragment (Version/StartHTML/EndHTML headers) — Clip.Core stores item.HtmlText; verify whether stored HtmlText is already CF_HTML-wrapped (standalone passes it straight to WPF TextDataFormat.Html which auto-wraps, so for WinRT use the toolkit helper Windows.ApplicationModel.DataTransfer.HtmlFormatHelper.CreateHtmlFormat(item.HtmlText) to guarantee a valid fragment). Then Clipboard.SetContent(data) + Clipboard.Flush() as today (lines 200-201). No new SDK surface needed for the copy path. For an optional in-palette Paste command (paletteApproach mentions 'The palette never pastes'): CommandPalette extensions run out-of-process and cannot reliably SendInput Ctrl+V into the prior foreground window the way the standalone shell does (it owns focus return via _returnFocusHwnd). Recommend NOT implementing synthetic paste in-process; instead, if a true Paste is desired, route it to the standalone helper exe via the existing ClipHistoryActionCommand process-launch path (ClipExecutableLocator + 'paste' arg) so the shell performs the focus-restore + SendKeys it already implements (MainWindow PasteSelected). The copy-with-formatting fix is the high-value, fully feasible part; the 'Paste as plain text' override is a second CommandContextItem that calls the copy writer with a forced PlainText preference.
implementationSteps: Extend the shared settings snapshot: in Clip.Core/ClipSharedSettings.cs add a PasteFormatPreference DefaultPasteFormat field to ClipSharedSettingsSnapshot (record struct at lines 18-21). In LoadFromJson (lines 47-54) read it with the existing EnumValue<PasteFormatPreference>(root, "DefaultPasteFormat", PasteFormatPreference.PlainText) — note EnumValue already handles both numeric and string JSON kinds, matching how Clip.Watcher/Program.cs:965-977 parses the same key. This reads from ClipStoragePaths.SettingsPath, the SAME settings.json the standalone shell writes via ApplyDefaultPasteFormat, so the palette automatically honors the user's choice with no new persistence.

Change the palette copy writer to honor the preference: in ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs ClipClipboardWriter.SetItem (lines 165-202), replace the hard-coded PasteFormatPreference.PlainText at line 174. Load the preference once via ClipSharedSettings.Load().DefaultPasteFormat (or accept it as a method parameter — see step 3). Compute effective = (preference == OriginalFormatting && ClipboardPasteData.HasOriginalFormatting(item)) ? OriginalFormatting : PlainText, then call ClipboardPasteData.Create(item, effective).

Populate HTML/RTF on the WinRT DataPackage: in the Text/Link/Color branch (lines 172-176) after data.SetText(payload.Text), add: if (payload.Html is not null) data.SetHtmlFormat(HtmlFormatHelper.CreateHtmlFormat(payload.Html)); and if (payload.Rtf is not null) data.SetRtf(payload.Rtf); This mirrors standalone SetClipboard (MainWindow.xaml.cs:4382-4398) which sets Unicode + Html + Rtf formats. The hydrated item passed in already carries HtmlText/RtfText because ClipHistoryActionCommand resolves it via _store.GetItem(_action.Arguments[1]) at line 101, so no re-hydration is required.

Thread a per-invocation override: give ClipClipboardWriter.SetItem an optional PasteFormatPreference? overrideFormat = null parameter. The default copy path (called at line 110) passes null → uses ClipSharedSettings preference. Add a second copy variant forcing PlainText for the 'paste as plain text' / 'copy as plain text' override.

Add the context command for the override: in ClipHistoryPage.cs CreateContextCommand (lines 215-231) or in the per-item MoreCommands assembly (lines 200-206), when item.HasOriginalFormatting is true AND the resolved default is OriginalFormatting, add a CommandContextItem titled 'Copy as plain text' (Icon ) that invokes a ClipHistoryActionCommand variant forcing PlainText. Gate it so it only appears when it changes behavior (formatted item + OriginalFormatting default), keeping the menu clean.

Update the Details 'Saved format' affordance for honesty: ClipHistoryPage.cs DetailRows (line 262-265) already shows 'Plain text + formatting' vs 'Plain text'. Optionally add a row showing the active paste behavior (e.g. 'Will copy: Original formatting' / 'Plain text') derived from ClipSharedSettings.Load().DefaultPasteFormat so the 'Formatted' tag (line 355-358) no longer implies formatting that was previously silently dropped.

(Optional, deferred) True in-palette Paste: if implementing, add a 'Paste' CommandContextItem that routes through the existing ClipHistoryActionCommand executable-launch path (Invoke → ClipExecutableLocator.Resolve, lines 33-54) invoking the standalone helper with a 'paste' verb so the shell's PasteSelected (MainWindow.xaml.cs:3841-3886) performs focus restore + SendKeys. Do NOT attempt SendInput from the extension process. Return CommandResult.Dismiss() so Command Palette closes and focus returns to the target app.

Build and smoke-test: dotnet build the solution. Manually verify: with DefaultPasteFormat=Original formatting set in standalone settings, copy a formatted item from the palette and paste into WordPad/Word — formatting is preserved; with Plain text set, only text pastes; the 'Copy as plain text' override always drops formatting; plain-only items are unaffected.
blueprints.responsivenessNotes: ClipSharedSettings.Load() does a small synchronous file read of settings.json; call it once per copy invocation (inside Invoke/SetItem), not during list rendering, so GetItems stays fast and the cached _cachedItems path in ClipHistoryPage is untouched. The copy path already runs on Invoke (user action), so the extra read and SetHtmlFormat/SetRtf calls are negligible and synchronous-by-design (clipboard APIs must complete before Clipboard.Flush). Do not add the paste-format read to CreateListItem/CreateTags hot loops. Use HtmlFormatHelper.CreateHtmlFormat to avoid malformed CF_HTML that some targets silently reject. Keep the override context command conditional so the More-commands menu does not grow for items where it has no effect. Reuse Clip.Core ClipboardPasteData (no reimplementation of HTML/RTF selection). Icons use existing Segoe Fluent glyphs (\\uE8C8 copy) for native look; no new assets.

blueprints.feature: Append to clipboard (accumulate text) for Text/Link items in Command Palette
blueprints.sdkNotes: Mirror the existing in-process "copy" branch in ClipHistoryActionCommand.TryInvokeInProcess (ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:108-113). The class already imports Windows.ApplicationModel.DataTransfer (DataPackage/Clipboard) and the Toolkit. READ current clipboard with WinRT: var view = Clipboard.GetContent(); string existing = view.Contains(StandardDataFormats.Text) ? view.GetTextAsync().AsTask().GetAwaiter().GetResult() : string.Empty; (StandardDataFormats is in Windows.ApplicationModel.DataTransfer). WRITE with the same pattern ClipClipboardWriter.SetItem uses (lines 167-201): new DataPackage { RequestedOperation = Copy }; data.SetText(existing + payload); Clipboard.SetContent(data); Clipboard.Flush(). Get the item plain-text payload via Clip.Core ClipboardPasteData.Create(item, PasteFormatPreference.PlainText).Text (same source the copy path uses at line 174) instead of reimplementing TextPayload. Return CommandResult.Dismiss() on success, CommandResult.KeepOpen()+ToastStatusMessage on error, matching the copy branch. The action surfaces via ClipHistoryPage.CreateContextCommand whose default switch branch already routes unknown action ids to new ClipHistoryActionCommand(action, store, InvalidateItems) (ClipHistoryPage.cs:223) so no routing change is needed there; it appears in the item MoreCommands (Ctrl+K) flyout. Do NOT route append through ClipboardHistoryActionExecutor (that path is only for store mutations that set MutatedHistory); append touches only the OS clipboard, so handle it inline alongside copy/open/reveal. The synchronous .AsTask().GetAwaiter().GetResult() on GetTextAsync is consistent with existing codebase usage (line 179) and acceptable because Invoke runs off the render thread.
implementationSteps: 1. In Clip.Core/ClipboardHistoryListCommand.cs, ClipboardHistoryListAction.ForHistoryItem (line 171): after the edit-text block (lines 192-200), add an 'append' action gated for Text OR Link items (broader than the Watcher Text-only rule, per the gap spec). Use: if (item.Kind is ClipboardItemKind.Text or ClipboardItemKind.Link && (!string.IsNullOrEmpty(item.Text) || !string.IsNullOrEmpty(item.Preview))) actions.Add(new ClipboardHistoryListAction(Id: "append", Label: "Append to Clipboard", Executable: "Clip.Watcher.exe", Arguments: ["append", item.Id], RequiresFullItem: true)). RequiresFullItem: true so the palette resolves the full item (with .Text) before invoking, matching copy/edit-text.

2. (Optional but recommended) In Clip.Watcher/Program.cs AppendText (line 285-295), relax the Text-only guard to allow Link as well (item.Kind is ClipboardItemKind.Text or ClipboardItemKind.Link) so the executable fallback path stays consistent with the new Link support. Without this, a Link append would fail only when the in-process path is unavailable (store null). Low risk, keeps parity.

3. In Clip.CommandPalette/ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs TryInvokeInProcess: extend the command allow-list at lines 94-98 to include "append" (so it does not early-return false). Then in the item-resolved block after line 106, add a branch: if (command.Equals("append", StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase)) { ClipClipboardWriter.AppendItem(item); result = CommandResult.Dismiss(); return true; }. Reuses the existing _store.GetItem(_action.Arguments[1]) lookup at line 101 (returns the full item incl. Text), and the existing try/catch -> ShowError path.

4. In Clip.CommandPalette/ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs add a static AppendItem method to ClipClipboardWriter (alongside SetItem, ~line 165): read existing clipboard text via var view = Windows.ApplicationModel.DataTransfer.Clipboard.GetContent(); var existing = view.Contains(StandardDataFormats.Text) ? view.GetTextAsync().AsTask().GetAwaiter().GetResult() : string.Empty; var payload = ClipboardPasteData.Create(item, PasteFormatPreference.PlainText).Text; if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(payload)) throw new InvalidOperationException("Nothing to append."); var data = new DataPackage { RequestedOperation = DataPackageOperation.Copy }; data.SetText(existing + payload); Clipboard.SetContent(data); Clipboard.Flush(). Guard kind to Text/Link, throwing InvalidOperationException otherwise (surfaced as a toast).

5. Add the 'append' icon to BOTH IconForAction switch maps (ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:145-160 and ClipHistoryPage.cs:405-420): "append" => "" (Add glyph) or "" (clipboard-with-add); pick one and use it in both so the list-item flyout and the command both show a consistent icon.

6. Add the StandardDataFormats import if not already pulled in transitively (it lives in Windows.ApplicationModel.DataTransfer, already imported at ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:5, so no new using is required).

7. Update/extend the existing ForHistoryItem unit tests (search the test project for tests asserting the action set for Text/Link items) to assert an 'append' action is produced for Text and Link and absent for Image/Files/Color; verifies the gate and ordering.
blueprints.responsivenessNotes: Append runs entirely in-process via WinRT Clipboard (no Clip.Watcher.exe process spawn) on the same code path as the existing copy action, so it is instantaneous and consistent with the native copy experience. The GetTextAsync/SetContent calls are synchronous-blocked with .AsTask().GetAwaiter().GetResult() exactly as the existing image copy does (line 179); this runs on the command Invoke thread, not the list render thread, so it does not stall ClipHistoryPage.GetItems. No history mutation occurs, so InvalidateItems / RaiseItemsChanged is intentionally NOT called (append changes only the OS clipboard, not the store) keeping the list cache valid and avoiding a needless re-query. Errors surface through the existing ToastStatusMessage helper (non-blocking native toast) and the command returns KeepOpen on failure / Dismiss on success, matching copy. Reusing ClipboardPasteData.Create and ClipClipboardWriter means no new clipboard-format logic and no duplicated formatting behavior. Icon uses a Segoe Fluent glyph consistent with the existing action icons for a native look.

blueprints.sdkNotes: The palette is an out-of-process windowless COM server (DynamicListPage in ClipHistoryPage.cs), so there is NO owned HWND like the WPF shell's WindowInteropHelper(this).Handle used at MainWindow.xaml.cs:5266. DataTransferManager.ShowShareUIForWindow requires a valid foreground HWND; obtain it via P/Invoke user32!GetForegroundWindow() (the CmdPal host window is foreground when a context command fires) and pass that to IDataTransferManagerInterop.GetForWindow. Reuse the exact interop shape already proven in Clip.Shell/WindowsShareService.cs:12-81 (DataTransferManagerId GUID, IDataTransferManagerInterop with GUID 3A3DCD6C-..., GetForWindow + ShowShareUIForWindow, MarshalInterface<DataTransferManager>.FromAbi). The WinRT types are already available to the palette project: ClipCopyPathCommand.cs:3 already `using Windows.ApplicationModel.DataTransfer` and ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:6 uses Windows.Storage; the csproj targets net9.0-windows10.0.22000.0 with CsWinRT, so DataTransferManager, StorageFile, DataPackage all resolve with no new package. Implement Share/Blip as InvokableCommand subclasses (same base as ClipCopyPathCommand.cs:7) returning CommandResult.KeepOpen() (Share UI is async; keep the palette open) or CommandResult.ShowToast on failure, surfacing errors via ToastStatusMessage exactly like ClipShellActionCommand.cs:64. Wire both as CommandContextItem entries inside ClipHistoryPage.CreateContextCommand (ClipHistoryPage.cs:215-231) so they appear in the per-item MoreCommands flyout next to copy/pin/delete, gated by item kind. Reuse Clip.Core verbatim: ClipboardSharePayload.Create (ClipboardSharePayload.cs:19) and Cleanup (line 50), BlipShareLaunchPlan.IsInstalled (BlipShareLaunchPlan.cs:16) and Create (line 37). Resolve the full ClipboardHistoryItem from the list item via store.GetItem(item.Id) (already used at ClipItemPreviewPage.cs:26) because Create needs the full item, not the ClipboardHistoryListItem. DataTransferManager.IsSupported() (WindowsShareService.cs:14) gates whether to add the Share command at all. SDK limit: there is no IContextItem submenu nesting in this SDK version, so present Share and Blip as two sibling context items rather than a nested 'Share' submenu like the standalone app.
implementationSteps: Add CommandPaletteShareService.cs: a static helper that ports Clip.Shell/WindowsShareService.cs into the palette project. Copy the DataTransferManagerId GUID, the IDataTransferManagerInterop ComImport interface (WindowsShareService.cs:74-81), and the ShowShareUI body (lines 24-71) verbatim. Replace the hwnd parameter source: add `[DllImport("user32.dll")] static extern IntPtr GetForegroundWindow();` and call it internally so callers need no HWND. Keep IsSupported() => DataTransferManager.IsSupported(). Keep the DataRequested handler that sets Title/Description, SetText for Text/Link/Color, SetStorageItems for files, and wires ShareCompleted/ShareCanceled to payload.Cleanup() (WindowsShareService.cs:55-56).

Add ClipShareCommand.cs: InvokableCommand (mirror ClipCopyPathCommand.cs:7). Constructor takes ClipboardHistoryStore + item id (string). Name = "Share", Icon = new IconInfo("") (Share glyph). In Invoke(): resolve full item via store.GetItem(id) (pattern from ClipItemPreviewPage.cs:26); if null show ToastStatusMessage error and return KeepOpen. Build payload = ClipboardSharePayload.Create(item) (ClipboardSharePayload.cs:19) inside try/catch — Create throws InvalidOperationException('This item cannot be shared.') which becomes a toast. Compute title/description by porting ShareTitle/ShareDescription (MainWindow.xaml.cs:5317-5337). Call CommandPaletteShareService.ShowShareUI(item, payload, title, description, onDataFailed) where onDataFailed shows a toast. On any catch, call payload?.Cleanup() (matches MainWindow.xaml.cs:5278) and toast. Return CommandResult.KeepOpen() (Share UI is modal/async; do not Dismiss).

Add ClipBlipShareCommand.cs: InvokableCommand. Name = "Share with Blip", Icon = new IconInfo("") or a distinct glyph. In Invoke(): resolve item via store.GetItem(id); payload = ClipboardSharePayload.Create(item); plan = BlipShareLaunchPlan.Create(payload) (BlipShareLaunchPlan.cs:37). Start process with ProcessStartInfo { FileName = BlipShareLaunchPlan.ExecutableName, UseShellExecute = true } and add plan.LaunchArguments via startInfo.ArgumentList (preferred over manual quoting — ArgumentList handles escaping, unlike the QuoteProcessArgument hack at MainWindow.xaml.cs:5312-5315). Process.Start. Note: payload for Text/Link/Color creates a temp file (HasTemporaryFiles true) that Blip reads asynchronously, so do NOT Cleanup immediately (matches shell behavior at MainWindow.xaml.cs:5300 which leaves temp files for the daily CleanupStaleTemporaryFilesIfDue pass). On success return CommandResult.Dismiss(); on exception toast error and KeepOpen.

Modify ClipHistoryPage.CreateContextCommand (ClipHistoryPage.cs:215-231): after building the existing action-based CommandContextItem list, append a Share command for every shareable item and a Blip command when installed. Simplest: change CreateListItem (ClipHistoryPage.cs:200-207) so that after `var moreCommands = item.Actions.Select(...)`, it concatenates extra share commands before assigning listItem.MoreCommands. Add a private helper BuildShareCommands(item, store) returning IEnumerable<CommandContextItem>: if CommandPaletteShareService.IsSupported() yield a CommandContextItem(new ClipShareCommand(store, item.Id)) { Title="Share", Icon=new IconInfo("") }; if BlipShareLaunchPlan.IsInstalled() yield CommandContextItem(new ClipBlipShareCommand(store, item.Id)) { Title="Share with Blip", Icon=... }.

Gate by shareability so the action never shows a guaranteed-fail toast: only emit Share/Blip for kinds ClipboardSharePayload.Create can handle. Add a static IsShareable(ClipboardHistoryListItem) check mirroring ClipboardSharePayload.Create branches (ClipboardSharePayload.cs:21-45): Text/Link/Color always shareable (temp file written); Image shareable only when AssetPath exists; Files shareable only when at least one path in item.FilePaths still exists on disk. Use this in BuildShareCommands.

Add an IconForAction entry / reuse glyphs: add "share" => "" to the IconForAction switches (ClipHistoryPage.cs:405-420 and ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:145-160) only if you route share through action ids; since these are standalone commands you can hardcode the IconInfo in the command constructors instead — no switch change needed.

Cache invalidation: BlipShareLaunchPlan.IsInstalled() and IsShareable both read filesystem state. Since GetItems caches by index stamp (ClipHistorypage.cs:80-95), the Blip-installed check is evaluated at item-build time and will refresh whenever history changes; acceptable. Do NOT call IsInstalled() inside a hot loop per item more than once — hoist it to a single bool in CreateItems (ClipHistoryPage.cs:121) and pass it down, so PATH scanning (BlipShareLaunchPlan.cs:26-34) runs once per refresh, not once per row.

Build and verify: `dotnet build src/Clip.CommandPalette/Clip.CommandPalette.csproj`. Manually verify in Command Palette: open Clip history, select a text item, open More commands, confirm 'Share' opens the Windows share sheet anchored to the CmdPal window, and that ShareCompleted/Cancel deletes the temp file. With blip.exe on PATH confirm 'Share with Blip' appears and launches; without it, confirm the command is absent.

Optional polish: in ClipCommandsProvider or extension startup, call ClipboardSharePayload.CleanupStaleTemporaryFilesIfDue() (ClipboardSharePayload.cs:58) once per process launch so palette-created temp share files are reaped, matching the shell. This keeps temp dir hygiene even when the WPF shell never runs.
blueprints.responsivenessNotes: Share UI invocation is on the COM/UI thread of the command invoke; the DataRequested handler is already async with a deferral (WindowsShareService.cs:29-67) so StorageFile.GetFileFromPathAsync runs without blocking. Do not block on the share completing — return CommandResult.KeepOpen() immediately after ShowShareUIForWindow so the palette stays responsive. Resolve full item lazily via store.GetItem only inside Invoke(), never during GetItems list building, so the list stays fast. Hoist BlipShareLaunchPlan.IsInstalled() to one call per CreateItems refresh (it scans PATH directories) rather than per row. Gate Share/Blip visibility with IsShareable + IsSupported + IsInstalled so users never see an action that is guaranteed to fail — feels native. Use Segoe MDL2 glyph \\uE72D (Share) for the Share command icon to match CmdPal's built-in iconography. Reuse Clip.Core (ClipboardSharePayload, BlipShareLaunchPlan) verbatim — no payload/cleanup logic is reimplemented, so behavior matches the standalone shell exactly including temp-file lifetime. Errors surface through ToastStatusMessage/CommandResult.ShowToast, the same non-blocking pattern already used across the palette.

blueprints.feature: Open With… searchable app picker (secondary DynamicListPage) for image/file/path clipboard items in Command Palette
blueprints.sdkNotes: Use a secondary page pushed from a CommandContextItem. Build it as a ListPage subclass (Microsoft.CommandPalette.Extensions.Toolkit) — not DynamicListPage. App discovery returns a fixed in-memory list; the palette's built-in list filtering already gives instant native fuzzy search over Title/Subtitle, so override GetItems() to return the discovered app rows once and let the host filter. (DynamicListPage is only worth it if you want to drive your own filtering via UpdateSearchText, which is unnecessary here.) Each app is an IListItem (ListItem) whose primary ICommand is a new InvokableCommand that calls Process.Start(ClipboardItemLaunchCommand.CreateOpenStartInfo(fullItem, appPath)) for exe apps, or packaged-app activation (IApplicationActivationManager.ActivateForFile) for AppUserModelId apps. Set ListItem.Title=app name, Subtitle=source (Default app / Recommended / Recent / Start Menu / Store app), Icon=new IconInfo(Segoe glyph) keyed by source — do NOT port StartMenuIconLookup (it returns System.Drawing.Image which requires WinForms and is unavailable in the net8.0 palette; use glyphs instead, e.g.  app,  default,  recent,  store). Wire the entry point as a CommandContextItem(new ClipOpenWithPage(...)) in ClipHistoryPage.CreateContextCommand and ClipItemPreviewPage.CreateContextCommand, gated to items that have an open target. Existing launch plumbing already supports this: ClipboardItemLaunchCommand.CreateOpenStartInfo(item, appPath) (Clip.Core/ClipboardItemLaunchCommand.cs:7) takes the optional appPath; ClipHistoryActionCommand already passes Arguments[2] as the app path for the 'open' verb (ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:116); and Clip.Command already documents 'open <id> [app path]' (Clip.Command/Program.cs:216). Return CommandResult.Dismiss() after a successful launch, CommandResult.KeepOpen() + ToastStatusMessage on error (mirror ClipHistoryActionCommand.ShowError, ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:136). For packaged-app COM activation, marshal-release per the existing pattern in PackagedAppLauncher (Clip.Watcher/Program.cs:3159).
implementationSteps: Retarget Clip.Core to net8.0-windows10.0.22000.0 (currently net8.0 per Clip.Core.csproj). The discovery code needs Microsoft.Win32.Registry, Shlwapi AssocQueryString P/Invoke, and shell COM activation — all need a Windows TFM. Verify the palette (net9.0-windows10.0.22000.0) and Watcher still reference it cleanly. This is the one load-bearing build change; confirm no other Clip.Core consumer breaks.

Create Clip.Core/OpenWithAppDiscovery.cs as a public static service by extracting the WinForms-free discovery logic from Clip.Watcher/Program.cs: the public record AppChoice (Program.cs:2158), AppDiscovery.GetApps/DesktopApps/IsUsefulOpenWithApp/NormalizedAppName/BestChoice/AddAssociatedApp/StartMenuApps/PackagedApps/AppPathRegistryApps (Program.cs:2189-2392), ShortcutResolver (Program.cs:2394), AssociationQuery + AssociationString (Program.cs:3536-3573), PackagedAppDiscovery + PackagedAppInfo + StartAppJson (Program.cs:3104-3157), and PackagedAppLauncher + its COM interop interfaces (Program.cs:3159-3247). Make AppChoice and GetApps public; keep the rest internal to Clip.Core. Do NOT move StartMenuIconLookup (System.Drawing/WinForms-bound) — the palette will use Segoe glyphs.

Create Clip.Core/OpenWithRecentStore.cs by moving OpenWithRecentStore (Program.cs:3459-3534) verbatim; make Load/Save public. It already persists per-extension recents to %LocalAppData%/Clip/open-with-recent.json keyed by extension (or '<folder>'), so both the standalone and palette share the same recent list automatically. Add a public AppLauncher.OpenWith(targetPath, AppChoice) (Program.cs:2160-2187) OR expose a method that returns the resolved appPath string so the palette can feed ClipboardItemLaunchCommand.CreateOpenStartInfo — prefer exposing both an exe-path launch and a packaged-activation launch.

In Clip.Watcher/Program.cs, delete the moved types and replace their usages with the new Clip.Core types (the Watcher already references Clip.Core). Keep StartMenuIconLookup in the Watcher (it stays WinForms-bound for the WPF picker). This guarantees one shared discovery code path, eliminating drift between surfaces.

Add a public helper to Clip.Core/ClipboardItemLaunchCommand.cs that exposes the open target for an item (e.g. public static string? GetOpenTarget(ClipboardHistoryItem item) wrapping the existing private OpenTarget at line 23), so the palette can (a) decide whether to show the Open With command and (b) pass the right path into discovery/recents. Also add an overload/path that, given an AppChoice with AppUserModelId, routes to packaged activation instead of CreateOpenStartInfo.

Create Clip.CommandPalette/ClipOpenWithLaunchCommand.cs: an InvokableCommand constructed with (ClipboardHistoryListItem listItem, ClipboardHistoryStore store, AppChoice app, string targetPath). Invoke(): resolve full item via store.GetItem(listItem.Id); if app.AppUserModelId is set call PackagedAppLauncher.OpenFile(app.AppUserModelId, targetPath); else Process.Start(ClipboardItemLaunchCommand.CreateOpenStartInfo(fullItem, app.IsDefault ? null : app.ExecutablePath)); on success call OpenWithRecentStore.Save(targetPath, app) and return CommandResult.Dismiss(); on exception show ToastStatusMessage(MessageState.Error) and return CommandResult.KeepOpen(). Mirror error handling from ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:136.

Create Clip.CommandPalette/ClipOpenWithPage.cs : ListPage. Constructor takes (ClipboardHistoryListItem item, ClipboardHistoryStore store, string targetPath). Set Title="Open with " + filename, Name="Open With", Icon=new IconInfo(""), ShowDetails=false. Override GetItems(): call OpenWithAppDiscovery.GetApps(targetPath) on first access, cache the result, and map each AppChoice to a ListItem(new ClipOpenWithLaunchCommand(item, store, app, targetPath)){ Title=app.Name, Subtitle=app.Source, Icon=IconForSource(app), Section= app.IsDefault||app.IsRecent ? "Recommended" : "All apps" }. Run the first discovery off the constructor thread — kick it in a Task and RaiseItemsChanged() when ready, returning a single 'Loading apps…' placeholder ListItem(new NoOpCommand()) until then (GetApps spawns powershell Get-StartApps which can take up to 3.5s, Program.cs:3132). The static _desktopAppCache in AppDiscovery already makes subsequent opens instant.

Wire the entry point in ClipHistoryPage.CreateContextCommand (ClipHistoryPage.cs:215) and ClipItemPreviewPage.CreateContextCommand (ClipItemPreviewPage.cs:38): when ClipboardItemLaunchCommand.GetOpenTarget(fullItem) is non-null (i.e. Image with AssetPath, Files, or path-like Text — matches HasOpenWithTarget logic in MainWindow.xaml.cs:1503), add new CommandContextItem(new ClipOpenWithPage(item, store, targetPath)){ Title="Open with…", Icon=new IconInfo("") } to MoreCommands. Note ClipHistoryPage builds context commands from item.Actions only today, so add this as an extra appended command rather than via the action switch. Add the 'open-with' glyph to both IconForAction maps for consistency.

Build the solution; manually verify: copy an image and a file, open Command Palette, invoke Open With… on each, confirm the picker shows Default/Recommended/Recent/Start Menu/Store apps, type-to-filter is instant, Enter launches the app with the file, the choice appears as Recent on next open, and that text/color/link items without a path do NOT show the command.
blueprints.responsivenessNotes: The only slow operation is packaged-app enumeration via powershell Get-StartApps (WaitForExit 3500ms, Program.cs:3132). Do NOT call GetApps() on the page constructor or UI thread: render a 'Loading apps…' placeholder immediately, run discovery on a background Task, then RaiseItemsChanged(). AppDiscovery._desktopAppCache and PackagedAppDiscovery._cache are process-static, so the second and later opens are instant within a palette session. Recents and the default/recommended app come from fast local sources (registry, file associations, Start Menu .lnk walk) and can populate the 'Recommended' section first while the Store apps stream in. Native search/filtering is delegated to the Command Palette host by using ListPage (no custom UpdateSearchText needed). Use Segoe Fluent glyph IconInfo for app rows rather than extracting real per-app bitmaps — avoids pulling System.Drawing/WinForms into the lightweight palette process and keeps the list snappy; richer per-app icons can be a later enhancement. Errors surface via non-blocking ToastStatusMessage, matching existing palette commands.

blueprints.feature: Time-bucket grouping (Today / Yesterday / This week / This month / This year / Older) of unpinned clipboard history into Command Palette section headers below Pinned
blueprints.sdkNotes: Uses only already-in-use SDK surface: ListItem.Section (string) drives section headers in the Microsoft.CommandPalette.Extensions list UI. The shell already proves the same six buckets work conceptually (MainWindow.xaml.cs:3776-3782 yields "Pinned items","Today","Yesterday","This week","This month","This year","Older"). In CmdPal, section header ORDER follows the order in which each distinct Section string FIRST appears in the returned IListItem[] — there is no explicit section-ordering API. So the IListItem[] from ClipHistoryPage.CreateItems must be assembled bucket-by-bucket in recency order (Pinned, Today, ..., Older); do NOT rely on the list being re-sorted by the host. No new SDK types needed: keep returning IListItem[] from GetItems()/CreateItems, keep DynamicListPage, ShowDetails, Filters, MoreCommands exactly as-is. Section is a plain settable string on ListItem (Microsoft.CommandPalette.Extensions.Toolkit.ListItem), already set today at ClipHistoryPage.cs:196 via SectionFor (ClipHistoryPage.cs:212-213). No IContextItem/IDetails/Forms changes. Empty buckets must be skipped (don't emit items for them) so no empty headers appear — natural consequence of grouping then concatenating non-empty groups.

blueprints.sdkNotes: The SDK exposes ONE filter dropdown per page via the `Filters` (concrete `Microsoft.CommandPalette.Extensions.Toolkit.Filters`, implementing `IFilters`) assigned to `DynamicListPage.Filters`. It has a single `CurrentFilterId` string and raises `PropChanged` when the user changes selection. `GetFilters()` returns `IFilterItem[]`, which may mix `Filter` (id+Name+Icon) and `Separator` to draw grouped sections in the same dropdown. There is NO API for a second concurrent dropdown, so kind+date cannot be two independent native dropdowns. Approach: keep ONE dropdown but split it into two visually-separated sections with a `Separator` — a Type/Pinned section (existing 7) and a Date section (7 new: All time, Today, Yesterday, This week, This month, This year, Older). Encode date ids with a `date:` prefix (e.g. `date:today`) to keep them disjoint from kind ids. Because `CurrentFilterId` is a single value, hold the last-chosen kind id and last-chosen date id in two private fields on `ClipHistoryPage`, updated inside `Filters_PropChanged` based on which prefix the new id carries; the active kind+date pair is then both applied in `CreateItems`. `RaiseItemsChanged()` after each change re-renders. Reuse `ClipboardHistoryListFilter.Matches` for the kind axis and a new `DateMatches(dateId, item, today)` for the date axis (ported from MainWindow.xaml.cs:7399 DateKey). The `Filter.Icon` uses `IconInfo` segoe glyphs as the existing code already does. No async needed — filtering is in-memory over the already-loaded `result.Items`.

blueprints.feature: File-kind sub-filter (folder/pdf/excel/visio/html/image/text/word/powerpoint + discovered kinds) for the Files filter in the Clip Command Palette page
blueprints.sdkNotes: Command Palette's IFilters is a FLAT model: Filters() returns IFilterItem[] and there is a single CurrentFilterId (confirmed against the SDK spec - IFilter has Id/Name/Icon; ISeparatorFilterItem/toolkit Separator exists for grouping). There is NO native cascading/dropdown filter, so the standalone's File-pill dropdown (MainWindow.xaml.cs:6998 OnFileDropClick) must be modeled as additional FLAT filter entries that appear contextually. Approach: (1) Keep the existing top-level filters (All/Pinned/Text/Links/Files/Images/Colors from ClipHistoryFilters.cs:14). (2) When the active filter is Files OR any file sub-kind, append a Separator + one Filter per discovered file kind (Id like 'files:pdf', Name from LabelForFileKey, Icon via a kind->Segoe-glyph map). (3) ClipHistoryFilters.GetFilters() becomes dynamic: it reads the file kinds present in the latest result set, which ClipHistoryPage pushes into the filters object after each GetItems() build. The host re-calls GetFilters() and GetItems() whenever CurrentFilterId changes (DynamicListPage already wired via _filters.PropChanged -> InvalidateItems at ClipHistoryPage.cs:116); raising PropChanged on the Filters object re-renders the bar so the sub-kinds appear/disappear. (4) Classification must NOT reuse ClipboardHistoryStore.FileCategoryKey (ClipboardHistoryStore.cs:2059) directly - it is private static and in a different assembly. Lift it into a new PUBLIC static helper in Clip.Core (FileKindClassifier with Key(path) + Label(key)) mirroring standalone FileKindKey (MainWindow.xaml.cs:7414) and LabelForFileKey (7432), and have the store's private method delegate to it so logic stays single-sourced. Note: standalone's FileKindKey falls back to ext.TrimStart('.') for unknown kinds (so '.zip'->'zip') whereas store's FileCategoryKey returns 'other'; adopt the standalone TrimStart behavior in the shared helper so discovered kinds work, and keep 'other' only as the empty/no-ext bucket. Matching predicate: an item passes a 'files:KEY' filter iff item.Kind == 'Files' AND item.FilePaths.Any(p => FileKindClassifier.Key(p) == KEY). Use IconInfo(glyph) as already done in ClipHistoryFilters. Detail/preview pages are unaffected.

blueprints.sdkNotes: Render via Markdown, not WebView2 — live HTML/WebView2 cannot run inside a Command Palette extension. (1) List-pane preview is the existing `Details.Body` markdown built by `ClipHistoryPage.DetailsBody` (ClipHistoryPage.cs:300). Markdown supports fenced code blocks and `file://` images via the cmdpal query-string sizing hints already used in `ClipHistoryPage.ImageMarkdown` (cs:324) and `ClipImagePreviewContent.ImageMarkdown` (ClipImagePreviewContent.cs:27). (2) Rich preview page is `ClipItemPreviewPage : ContentPage` whose `GetContent()` returns `IContent[]` — today it returns `[ClipImagePreviewContent (MarkdownContent), ClipItemPreviewCard (FormContent)]` (ClipItemPreviewPage.cs:24-36). Add a new `MarkdownContent` for the rendered thumbnail there, mirroring `ClipImagePreviewContent.TryCreate`. (3) cmdpal image markdown only accepts a real file path/URI, so the renderer must yield a PNG ON DISK — which the standalone renderers already produce (pdftoppm writes `%LocalAppData%/Clip/pdf-previews/<hash>.png`, Office COM exports to `%LocalAppData%/Clip/document-previews/<hash>.{pdf,png}`); we just need the cached PNG path, never a System.Drawing.Image. (4) HARD CONSTRAINT: the renderers (`Clip.Watcher.PdfPreviewRenderer`, `Clip.Watcher.StaticDocumentPreviewRenderer` in Clip.Watcher/Program.cs:3010 and :2712) are `internal`, return `System.Drawing.Image`/`Bitmap`, and the project is net8.0-windows + UseWindowsForms=true + Office COM (dynamic, STA). Clip.CommandPalette is net9.0-windows, UseWPF=false, UseWindowsForms=false and CANNOT reference Clip.Watcher or System.Drawing as-is. So they must be refactored behind a path-returning facade. (5) Office/Visio rendering depends on installed Office (COM ProgIDs Word/Excel/PowerPoint/Visio.Application) and PDF rendering depends on pdftoppm.exe on PATH/LocalAppData — both can be absent, so every call must degrade to a placeholder. Use IconInfo glyphs already in ClipItemPreviewPage.IconFor for placeholder states.

All heavy rendering lives in ClipItemPreviewPage.GetContent, which only runs when the user opens the preview page (already the lazy boundary). Reuse the on-disk PNG cache fingerprinted by path+mtime+size+dpi so repeat opens are instant. Cap dpi at 120 for inline thumbnails and reuse the ?--x-cmdpal-fit/maxwidth/maxheight markdown hints so images are sized by the host. Use IconInfo glyphs for placeholder/unavailable states so it feels native, and guard COM/pdftoppm absence with graceful 'open in Clip' fallbacks rather than spinners that never resolve.

blueprints.feature: Original-formatting / RTF / HTML text preview fidelity
blueprints.sdkNotes: Command Palette content is Adaptive Cards (FormContent.TemplateJson here) rendered by the host - it cannot host WebView2, RTF, or arbitrary HTML, so true rich rendering (fonts, colors, embedded images) is out of scope by design; matching standalone's WPF RichTextBox fidelity is impossible in-palette. What IS achievable natively: (1) Adaptive Card TextBlock supports a limited markdown subset (bold **, italic _, bullet lists via '- ', ordered lists, and line breaks via \n\n) - so a structure-preserving plain-text rendering derived from HtmlText (paragraphs, headings, list bullets, links as 'text (url)') reads far better than today's flat monospace dump. (2) TextBlock 'maxLines' (currently 30 at ClipItemPreviewCard.cs:190) caps height; raise/remove it and rely on the host scroll, or keep a cap but append an explicit '...[N more lines]' marker so truncation is visible. (3) Full text is already hydrated server-side via store.GetItem in ClipItemPreviewPage.cs:26 then HydrateRichTextFromAsset (ClipboardHistoryStore.cs:1769-1770) populates HtmlText/RtfText, so no truncation below standalone's limit. blueprints.feature: Expand image to full overlay with pan/zoom
blueprints.sdkNotes: Microsoft.CommandPalette.Extensions has NO custom-canvas, pointer-event, or mouse-wheel API. IContent surfaces are limited to MarkdownContent, FormContent (Adaptive Cards), and TreeContent. Adaptive Cards 1.6 as hosted by CmdPal supports only the documented element set (Image, TextBlock, ColumnSet, FactSet, Action.Submit/Action.OpenUrl) with no scroll/zoom/drag hooks and no script. The only image sizing levers are the proprietary --x-cmdpal-fit/maxwidth/maxheight query hints already used in ClipImagePreviewContent.ImageMarkdown (ClipImagePreviewContent.cs:30). Therefore true in-palette wheel-zoom + drag-pan (standalone MainWindow.xaml.cs:5444 OnExpandImageClick, handlers 5614-5672) is NOT implementable. Native approximation: (1) image already renders at the 900x520 hint; (2) hand off to the OS viewer via the existing 'open' action, which routes through ClipboardItemLaunchCommand.CreateOpenStartInfo (Clip.Core/ClipboardItemLaunchCommand.cs:7, returns AssetPath for images, UseShellExecute=true) and is invoked in-process by ClipHistoryActionCommand.TryInvokeInProcess (ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:115). The 'open' action is already emitted by Core for images (ClipboardHistoryListCommand.cs:260-268, CanOpen at :315-318). Use IconInfo glyph  (FullScreen) or  (OpenFile) for the affordance; CommandContextItem already carries the open command into the preview page's Commands array (ClipItemPreviewPage.cs:21,38-54).
implementationSteps: Confirm the limitation is intrinsic: there is no SDK surface for wheel/drag canvas input. Treat this as a deliberate platform constraint, not a build target. The deliverable is a discoverable, native-feeling OS-viewer handoff plus an in-code note documenting why in-palette zoom/pan is impossible.

In ClipItemPreviewCard.BuildActions (ClipItemPreviewCard.cs:208-225), guarantee the 'open' action is surfaced as a primary inline Action.Submit for image items. It is already in the allow-list ({copy,pin,unpin,open,reveal}) and Take(4) — verify ordering so 'open' is not pushed out by other actions on images; if at risk, special-case images to put 'open' first.

Relabel the open affordance specifically for images so it reads as the expand substitute. In ClipItemPreviewPage.CreateContextCommand (ClipItemPreviewPage.cs:38-54) and/or in the card's inline action title, override the label to 'Open full size in image viewer' when fullItem.Kind == Image, instead of the generic 'Open'. Use IconInfo glyph  (FullScreen) for that case to signal the expand intent natively.

Add a one-line subtle hint in ClipItemPreviewCard.BuildPreviewItems image branch (ClipItemPreviewCard.cs:162-175): change 'Image preview is shown above.' to also tell the user how to get zoom/pan, e.g. 'Image preview shown above. Use Open full size to zoom and pan in your image viewer.' This makes the handoff discoverable rather than the feature looking missing.

In ClipImagePreviewContent.ImageMarkdown (ClipImagePreviewContent.cs:27-31), keep the 900x520 'fit' hint (already the largest practical inline size) and add a code comment documenting that wheel-zoom/drag-pan is intentionally delegated to the OS viewer because CmdPal exposes no custom canvas — referencing the standalone OnExpandImageClick handler so future readers understand the parity gap is by design.

Verify the open path is robust: ClipHistoryActionCommand.TryInvokeInProcess already handles 'open' in-process (ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:115-127) and returns CommandResult.Dismiss(); no change needed, but smoke-test that an image item with a valid AssetPath launches the default viewer and a missing/deleted AssetPath surfaces the existing ToastStatusMessage error (CanOpen only checks AssetPath non-empty, CreateOpenStartInfo re-checks File.Exists and returns null -> 'This item cannot be opened').

Optional polish: add an Action.OpenUrl alternative is NOT appropriate (file:// to a local asset is unreliable in the host); stick with the Action.Submit -> in-process Process.Start path already proven for 'open'.
blueprints.responsivenessNotes: Zero new I/O and no async needed — the only added work is string/label changes in template generation (synchronous, runs in GetContent which is already called per preview). The OS-viewer handoff uses the existing UseShellExecute=true ProcessStartInfo, which spawns the viewer process and returns immediately without blocking the palette UI; the command returns CommandResult.Dismiss() so the palette closes cleanly. No bitmap decoding happens in-process (the host renders the inline image from the file URI via the cmdpal hint). Use built-in Segoe Fluent glyphs (IconInfo) rather than loading custom image assets to stay native and avoid extra load. Keep the 900x520 'fit' hint so large images are downscaled by the host, not pre-loaded at full resolution.

blueprints.feature: Live history updates while the Command Palette page is open
blueprints.sdkNotes: DynamicListPage (Microsoft.CommandPalette.Extensions.Toolkit) already exposes the push mechanism this feature needs: RaiseItemsChanged(int) tells the host to re-call GetItems(), which is exactly what InvalidateItems() (ClipHistoryPage.cs:110-114) does today. The only missing piece is a *trigger* that isn't user input. Use a System.IO.FileSystemWatcher (System.IO) pointed at store.ContentRootPath (ClipboardHistoryStore.cs:72) filtering history.json / the two index files; on Changed/Created/Renamed, marshal back and call InvalidateItems(). FileSystemWatcher.Changed fires on a ThreadPool thread, and RaiseItemsChanged is safe to call from any thread (the toolkit raises the WinRT ItemsChanged event which the host marshals), but keep the handler tiny. Set IsLoading=true briefly only if a reload is heavy; here GetItems reads the top-index cache so it's cheap. There is NO host-provided Dispose/OnClosed callback for a Page in this SDK — the page object is a long-lived COM singleton created once in ClipCommandsProvider.TopLevelCommands() (ClipCommandsProvider.cs:25, cached in _commands). So the watcher will live for the whole extension process lifetime; that is acceptable (one watcher, idle until files change) but should be created lazily and guarded. Reuse CurrentIndexStampUtc (ClipHistoryPage.cs:165-174) inside GetItems' existing cache check so a watcher event that doesn't actually change the stamp results in a no-op reload (already handled at lines 81-87).
implementationSteps: Create ClipHistoryWatcher.cs: a small IDisposable wrapping a FileSystemWatcher on the store's ContentRootPath (ClipboardHistoryStore.ContentRootPath, ClipboardHistoryStore.cs:72). Constructor takes (string contentRootPath, Action onChanged). Set Filter not usable for multiple names, so set NotifyFilter = LastWrite | FileName | Size and watch the whole folder, then in the handler check e.Name is one of history.json / the HistoryIndexFileName / HistoryTopIndexFileName before invoking onChanged. Set EnableRaisingEvents=true. Wrap creation in try/catch (folder may not exist yet on first run) and expose a Rearm() that re-points if the dir gets created.

Add debounce inside ClipHistoryWatcher: clipboard captures rewrite history.json + index files in quick succession (see the temp-file + File.Move pattern at ClipboardHistoryStore.cs:920-945, 1208-1234), which fires multiple FSW events. Use a System.Threading.Timer reset to ~150-250ms on each event; only call onChanged once the burst settles. This prevents a thundering reload mid-write and avoids reading a half-written file.

In ClipHistoryPage.cs add a private ClipHistoryWatcher? _watcher field and a private bool _watcherStarted. Add a private EnsureWatcher() method that lazily creates the watcher on first GetItems() call (when _store.Value is materialized so ContentRootPath is known), passing InvalidateItems as the onChanged callback. Call EnsureWatcher() near the top of GetItems() right after 'var store = _store.Value;' (ClipHistoryPage.cs:79). Lazy-start avoids spinning up a watcher before the user ever opens the page.

Make the onChanged path safe and cheap: the watcher calls InvalidateItems() (ClipHistoryPage.cs:110), which nulls _cachedItems and calls RaiseItemsChanged(). Because GetItems re-checks CurrentIndexStampUtc (ClipHistoryPage.cs:80-87), a spurious event whose stamp matches the cached stamp will still rebuild once (cache was nulled). To keep it truly redundant-safe, gate InvalidateItems from the watcher behind a stamp compare: store last-seen stamp, and only null+raise if CurrentIndexStampUtc moved. Implement this as a new InvalidateIfStoreChanged() helper so manual invalidations (rename/edit/delete/clear) still force a hard refresh unconditionally.

Implement IDisposable on ClipHistoryPage to dispose _watcher (FileSystemWatcher implements IDisposable and holds an OS handle). Note: the SDK does not guarantee a Dispose call on the page, but implementing it is correct hygiene and lets ClipCommandPaletteExtension.Dispose (ClipCommandPaletteExtension.cs:25) optionally chain-dispose the provider/page on shutdown. Optionally have the watcher set EnableRaisingEvents=false when the page hasn't been queried recently, but given it's a singleton, simplest correct behavior is keep it enabled for process lifetime.

Verify the watched filenames resolve from the store, not hardcoded: read HistoryFilePath, HistoryIndexFilePath, HistoryTopIndexFilePath (ClipboardHistoryStore.cs:76-80) and compare e.FullPath against those (case-insensitive) inside the watcher handler so the design tracks any future filename/path changes automatically.

Manual test: open the palette History page, leave it open, copy several new items via Clip.exe, and confirm new entries appear in the Recent Items section without typing or re-filtering. Test rapid copies (debounce), copy while a filter is active (filter still applied because GetItems re-runs ClipboardHistoryListFilter.Matches, ClipHistoryPage.cs:124), and confirm pinned ordering and details still render. Confirm no exception when ContentRootPath doesn't exist yet.
blueprints.responsivenessNotes: Push, don't poll: a single FileSystemWatcher is near-zero cost when idle, far better than a polling timer for 'feels native/live'. Debounce burst writes (150-250ms) so the multi-file temp+move write sequence (ClipboardHistoryStore.cs:920-945) triggers exactly one reload, not several. Keep the GetItems reload cheap by relying on the existing top-index fast path and the CurrentIndexStampUtc cache guard (ClipHistoryPage.cs:80-87) so redundant watcher events are no-ops. Marshal the watcher callback off the ThreadPool only as far as calling InvalidateItems (lightweight: null cache + RaiseItemsChanged); do not do JSON parsing on the watcher thread. Lazy-create the watcher on first page query so the extension process stays idle until the user actually opens History. Gate the watcher-driven invalidation behind a stamp compare so background noise (e.g. asset cleanup writes) doesn't cause visible list churn or selection loss while the user is reading.

blueprints.feature: Duplicate Information rendering on details (FactSet + markdown table)
blueprints.sdkNotes: The SDK Details type (Microsoft.CommandPalette.Extensions.Toolkit) exposes Title, Body (markdown string), and Metadata (IDetailsElement[]). CreateDetails at ClipHistoryPage.cs:233-248 already populates Metadata with DetailsElement/DetailsLink rows built from DetailRows(item) — this is the native FactSet that Command Palette renders as a structured, frozen key/value panel in the details pane (mirrors the standalone's single Information panel). The Body markdown is rendered separately below/around the metadata. The duplication is purely self-inflicted: DetailsBody (300-317) re-emits the same DetailRows as a markdown '| Field | Value |' table. Fix is entirely within the extension layer — no Clip.Core changes, no new SDK surfaces. Keep DetailRows feeding Metadata (it is the single source of truth for the FactSet). The Metadata FactSet is the native, theme-aware presentation; the markdown table is not. No async/loading concerns since CreateDetails is synchronous and unchanged in shape.
implementationSteps: In ClipHistoryPage.cs DetailsBody (lines 300-317), remove the '## Information' section: delete the body.AppendLine() blank line, the body.AppendLine("## Information"), the blank line, the '| Field | Value |' header, the '| --- | --- |' separator, and the foreach over DetailRows(item) that emits '| {EscapeMarkdownCell(row.Key)} | {EscapeMarkdownCell(row.Value)} |'. Keep only the '## Preview' section (303-305) and the final return body.ToString().Trim() (316).

Resulting DetailsBody should build only: body.AppendLine("## Preview"); body.AppendLine(); body.AppendLine(IsPreviewableImage(item) ? ImageMarkdown(item.AssetPath!) : PreviewText(item)); then return body.ToString().Trim(). This mirrors the standalone: one Preview region in the Body, one frozen Information panel via the native Metadata FactSet.

Remove the now-dead EscapeMarkdownCell helper (lines 341-345) since its only callers were the deleted table rows (confirmed via grep: no other references in ClipHistoryPage.cs; the matches in ClipItemPreviewCard.cs / ClipImagePreviewContent.cs are independent private copies in different types and are untouched).
Leave DetailRows (250-298) and CreateDetails (233-248) exactly as-is — DetailRows remains the single source feeding the native Metadata FactSet, so all metadata fields still appear once, natively.

Build the Clip.CommandPalette project (dotnet build src/Clip.CommandPalette) to confirm no unused-symbol warnings-as-errors and that the StringBuilder simplification compiles; manually verify the details pane shows Preview text/image plus a single native Information FactSet with no duplicate markdown table.
blueprints.responsivenessNotes: Net reduction in work: the change removes a per-item StringBuilder loop over DetailRows (the markdown table) from DetailsBody, so building each list item's Details is slightly cheaper. The native Metadata FactSet is rendered by Command Palette's own UI, so it is automatically theme-aware (light/dark/high-contrast), uses native typography/spacing, and stays frozen/structured rather than reflowing as markdown — exactly the native feel the standalone has. No async or caching changes needed; CreateDetails stays synchronous and the existing GetItems cache (lines 81-95) is unaffected. DetailRows is still invoked once (for Metadata) instead of twice (Metadata + table), a minor allocation win per item.
