blueprints.sdkNotes:
Use Microsoft.CommandPalette.Extensions.Toolkit form-settings on the existing ClipSettingsPage (a ContentPage). Patterns already proven in ClipSettingsPage.cs: a single `Settings _settings` object, `_settings.Add(...)`, `GetContent() => _settings.ToContent()`, and one `_settings.SettingsChanged += OnSettingsChanged` handler.
- History limit / max item size / default paste format -> ChoiceSetSetting with the SAME label sets the standalone uses (HistoryLimit: 100/250/500/1000/Unlimited per MainWindow HistoryLimitRow:10388; MaxItemSize: 10/25/50/100 MB/Unlimited per MaxItemSizeRow:10411; PasteFormat: Plain text / Keep formatting mapping PasteFormatPreference in ClipboardPasteData.cs:3).

Surface these as informational text plus an InvokableCommand 'Open Clip.exe settings' (reuse ClipExecutableLocator + ClipShellActionCommand) — they legitimately belong to the WPF capture host.
- Persistence: extend ClipSharedSettings (Clip.Core/ClipSharedSettings.cs) with SetHistoryLimit/SetMaxItemSizeBytes/SetDefaultPasteFormat/SetClipboardFolderPath/ResetToDefaults using the existing JSON-merge `Update(Func<string,string>)` pattern (lines 92-98). This writes the same settings.json the watcher already reads (Clip.Watcher/Program.cs:843-899: ClipboardFolderPath/HistoryLimit/MaxItemSizeBytes/DefaultPasteFormat), so no new IPC is needed — the watcher re-reads via ReloadIfChanged() on every clipboard event (Program.cs:1917) and on its timer (1754).
Limits: the palette cannot host a native folder dialog, cannot rebind the global capture hotkey, and theme is host-owned — keep those WPF-only or read-only.

blueprints.responsivenessNotes: All writes go through the existing synchronous JSON-merge Update() which is sub-millisecond on the small settings.json — safe to run on the SettingsChanged callback thread. No IPC or restart needed: the watcher re-reads settings.json via ReloadIfChanged() on every clipboard event (Program.cs:1917) and timer (1754), so history-limit/max-size/paste-format changes take effect on the next copy. blueprints.feature: Save debug log snapshot / diagnostics (page-level "Save log snapshot" context command in the Clip history page)
blueprints.sdkNotes: Use IContextItem via CommandContextItem (Microsoft.CommandPalette.Extensions.Toolkit) wrapping an InvokableCommand, exactly as the existing page-level commands in ClipHistoryPage.CreateContextCommands() do. The new command extends InvokableCommand and returns ICommandResult from Invoke(): return CommandResult.Dismiss() on success (matches ClipShellActionCommand.Invoke line 55) or CommandResult.KeepOpen() on failure. Surface success/failure with ToastStatusMessage(new StatusMessage{ State = MessageState.Success/Error }) (toolkit), same helper as ClipShellActionCommand.ShowError (lines 64-71). Icon: IconInfo with a Segoe MDL2 glyph (e.g. "" diagnostic / "" page, or reuse ""/"" save-to-log). No Forms/Adaptive Cards, no IDetails, no IPage needed — this is a fire-and-forget InvokableCommand. CRITICAL CONSTRAINT: the snapshot writer (ShellLog at Clip.Shell/MainWindow.xaml.cs:11693 and WriteDebugSnapshot at 1676) reads live WPF UI state (ListScroll, ItemsHost, _selected, popup state) and lives in Clip.Shell, NOT Clip.Core — it cannot be moved to a shared Clip.Core writer without losing its meaning. Therefore DELEGATE to Clip.exe via --tray-action=save-log; do not reimplement. The executable already fully supports this: RunTrayAction switch case "save-log" (MainWindow.xaml.cs:1313) calls WriteDebugSnapshot("tray")+toast, and the already-running-instance path (App.xaml.cs:21-29 -> TrayActionRequest.Save) also handles it. So NO executable changes are required. Log file path for an optional "Reveal log" affordance is %LocalAppData%\Clip\shell.log (ShellLog.Path, MainWindow.xaml.cs:11699) — recompute it in the palette via Environment.SpecialFolder.LocalApplicationData + "Clip" + "shell.log" (same pattern as Clip.Core/ClipStoragePaths.cs), never by referencing Clip.Shell.
implementationSteps: In ClipShellActionCommand.cs, add a new static factory next to CheckForUpdates() (line 24): `public static ClipShellActionCommand SaveLogSnapshot() => new("Save Clip log snapshot", "", "--tray-action=save-log");`. This reuses the existing _trayAction delegation path (Invoke lines 44-54 add --palette-session then --tray-action=save-log) with zero new branching logic.

Optionally improve user feedback: in ClipShellActionCommand.Invoke(), after Process.Start succeeds for the save-log action, show a success toast naming the log path so the action confirms in-palette. Add a nullable `_successMessage` field set by the factory (e.g. "Log snapshot saved to %LocalAppData%\\Clip\\shell.log") and, when non-null, call ShowSuccess(_successMessage) before `return CommandResult.Dismiss();` (line 55). Add a ShowSuccess helper mirroring ShowError (lines 64-71) with State = MessageState.Success. Keep this generic so other actions are unaffected (leave their _successMessage null).

In ClipHistoryPage.CreateContextCommands() (lines 29-61), add a new CommandContextItem entry — place it after the CheckForUpdates item (line 46-50) and before the Clear-history commands so destructive items stay last: `new CommandContextItem(ClipShellActionCommand.SaveLogSnapshot()) { Title = "Save log snapshot", Icon = new IconInfo("") },`.

Verify the executable already advertises --tray-action=save-log handling (it does: NormalizeTrayAction + switch case "save-log" at MainWindow.xaml.cs:1313, and App.xaml.cs cross-instance TrayActionRequest.Save at line 28). No edits to Clip.Shell are required — confirm the case label matches the literal `save-log` exactly so NormalizeTrayAction (lower-invariant) routes correctly.

Build Clip.CommandPalette and smoke-test: open Command Palette -> Clip history page -> open page context menu (page-level More/context commands) -> invoke 'Save log snapshot'. Confirm (a) a Clip.exe palette-session process starts and writes a '=== Snapshot ===' block to %LocalAppData%\Clip\shell.log, (b) when Clip.exe is already running, the already-running instance handles it via TrayActionRequest without launching a duplicate, and (c) the palette shows a success toast and dismisses.

Consider deduping the four shell-delegation context items by extracting them into a small helper on ClipShellActionCommand (e.g. an array of (factory, title, icon) tuples) if the list grows; not required for this gap but keeps CreateContextCommands tidy.
blueprints.responsivenessNotes: The command is fire-and-forget: Process.Start returns immediately and the snapshot is written asynchronously by Clip.exe's ShellLog background writer thread (MainWindow.xaml.cs:11703), so the palette UI never blocks. Do NOT attempt to read/tail the log or wait for completion on the palette thread. Reuse the existing ClipExecutableLocator.Resolve(\"Clip.exe\") guard (ClipShellActionCommand line 28) so a missing executable shows a friendly error toast instead of throwing. Use native Segoe MDL2 IconInfo glyphs to match every other context item. Keep delegation identical to check-updates/settings so behavior (single-instance reuse via mutex + TrayActionRequest, --palette-session flag) is consistent and predictable. No extra processes are spawned when Clip.exe is already running — the duplicate exits fast after queuing the tray action. No new dependencies, no Clip.Core surface added, no caching needed.

blueprints.feature: Windows clipboard-history import trigger from the palette
blueprints.sdkNotes: Implement as a new InvokableCommand surfaced via CommandContextItem on ClipHistoryPage's page-level MoreCommands (the array returned by CreateContextCommands at ClipHistoryPage.cs:29, which ClipCommandsProvider.cs:32 already attaches as MoreCommands on the top-level CommandItem). InvokableCommand.Invoke() returns ICommandResult; use CommandResult.KeepOpen() so the palette stays open and the refreshed list is visible after import (same choice as ClipClearHistoryCommand.cs:43). Use ToastStatusMessage(new StatusMessage{...}).Show() for start/success/error feedback (pattern at ClipClearHistoryCommand.cs:29 and ClipShellActionCommand.cs:64); set State=MessageState.Error on failure. Icon: new IconInfo with a Segoe MDL2 glyph (e.g. download/sync glyph "" or ""), matching the glyph convention used throughout ClipHistoryPage.IconFor. DELEGATION over in-process: WindowsClipboardHistorySource is `internal sealed` and lives in the Clip.WindowsHistory WinExe (WindowsClipboardHistorySource.cs:10), not referenced by the palette, so true in-process ImportAsync would require making that class public and converting the helper to a library — out of scope. Instead launch the helper exe via System.Diagnostics.Process (ProcessStartInfo with UseShellExecute=false, CreateNoWindow=true, RedirectStandardOutput=true), exactly like ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:42 and Clip.Command/Program.cs:111. The helper already wires ClipboardHistoryImportService+WindowsClipboardHistorySource in-process at Clip.WindowsHistory/Program.cs:39 and prints the imported count to stdout, so parse stdout for the count to show in the toast. The palette already targets net9.0-windows10.0.22000.0 with WinRT (Clip.CommandPalette.csproj:14) and already uses Windows.ApplicationModel.DataTransfer, so a future in-process variant is possible but not needed now.
implementationSteps: Extend ClipExecutableLocator so it can find the Windows-history helper: add a public helper (or just call Resolve("Clip.WindowsHistory.exe")). Resolve already probes AppContext.BaseDirectory, the startup-registry exe directory, and the AppData/LocalAppData Programs\Clip install roots (ClipExecutableLocator.cs:13-54), which covers where Clip.WindowsHistory.exe ships alongside Clip.exe/Clip.Command.exe. No code change strictly required if you call Resolve directly; only add a named constant/method if you want clarity.

Create ClipImportWindowsHistoryCommand.cs as `internal sealed partial class ClipImportWindowsHistoryCommand : InvokableCommand`. Constructor takes `Action afterImport` (the page's InvalidateItems) plus an optional maxItems (default 120, matching Clip.Command/Program.cs:103 and Clip.WindowsHistory/Program.cs:37). Set Name="Import Windows clipboard history" and Icon=new IconInfo("").

In Invoke(): resolve the executable preferring ClipExecutableLocator.Resolve("Clip.WindowsHistory.exe") (owns the WinRT history read), then fall back to ClipExecutableLocator.Resolve("Clip.Command.exe") which re-delegates to the helper (Clip.Command/Program.cs:101). If neither resolves, ShowError("Clip's Windows clipboard-history helper was not found.") and return CommandResult.KeepOpen() (mirror ClipShellActionCommand.cs:30).

Run the helper off the UI thread to keep the palette responsive: build ProcessStartInfo{ FileName=helper, UseShellExecute=false, CreateNoWindow=true, RedirectStandardOutput=true, RedirectStandardError=true, WorkingDirectory=Path.GetDirectoryName(helper) }, add ArgumentList "import-windows-history","--max",max.ToString() (mirror Clip.Command/Program.cs:123-125). Start it, ReadToEnd stdout/stderr, WaitForExit. Do this inside a Task.Run / async-launched continuation so Invoke() returns immediately; optionally show an initial "Importing Windows clipboard history…" ToastStatusMessage before launching.

On completion: if ExitCode==0, int.TryParse(stdout.Trim()) to get the imported count and show ToastStatusMessage "Imported N item(s) from Windows clipboard history." (pluralize like ClipClearHistoryCommand.cs:31). If ExitCode!=0, show stderr (or a generic message) with State=MessageState.Error. Wrap in try/catch and route exceptions through ShowError (pattern ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:129).

After a successful import, invoke the afterImport callback to refresh the list. The callback is ClipHistoryPage.InvalidateItems (ClipHistoryPage.cs:110), which nulls _cachedItems and calls RaiseItemsChanged(); because GetItems re-derives its cache key from the history/index file timestamps (CurrentIndexStampUtc, ClipHistoryPage.cs:165), the newly imported rows show up. Marshal the callback/toast back appropriately since it fires from the background task.

Wire it into the page: add a new CommandContextItem(new ClipImportWindowsHistoryCommand(InvalidateItems)) to the array in ClipHistoryPage.CreateContextCommands() (ClipHistoryPage.cs:29), placed near the other helper/maintenance commands (e.g. before the Clear-history entries at ClipHistoryPage.cs:51), with Title "Import Windows clipboard history" and a matching Icon.

Build the solution (dotnet build on Clip.CommandPalette) and manually verify: open the Clip history page, trigger the new context command, confirm the toast reports a count and the list refreshes with imported items; confirm graceful error when the helper exe is absent and when Windows clipboard history is disabled (WindowsClipboardHistorySource.cs:14 returns empty, helper prints 0).
blueprints.responsivenessNotes: Never block the palette UI thread on the helper process. Launch via Process + RedirectStandardOutput inside a Task.Run/async continuation and return CommandResult.KeepOpen() immediately; show a 'Importing…' ToastStatusMessage up front and a result/error toast on completion (ToastStatusMessage is the toolkit's async-friendly notification, used at ClipClearHistoryCommand.cs:29). Pass --max 120 to bound work (default in both Program.cs files). Refresh purely via the page's InvalidateItems callback (RaiseItemsChanged) rather than rebuilding the page; the timestamp-based GetItems cache (ClipHistoryPage.cs:80-94) makes the refresh cheap and incremental. Keep CreateNoWindow=true so no console flashes. Use a Segoe MDL2 glyph IconInfo for a native look consistent with every other command on the page. Prefer the Clip.WindowsHistory.exe helper directly (one process) over Clip.Command.exe (which spawns the helper as a child, Clip.Command/Program.cs:111) to cut a hop.

blueprints.feature: More than 25 items / paging / configurable list limit
blueprints.sdkNotes: Page is a DynamicListPage (Microsoft.CommandPalette.Extensions.Toolkit). It already overrides GetItems() and UpdateSearchText(old,new) and calls RaiseItemsChanged() to repaint. Two viable paging surfaces: (A) Incremental load-more via the toolkit's IDynamicListPage paging contract: set HasMoreItems=true while the requested limit < ceiling, override LoadMore() to bump the page's effective limit and call RaiseItemsChanged(); Command Palette calls LoadMore() as the user scrolls near the bottom. (B) Simpler one-shot: just raise the limit to the ceiling and never page. Recommend (A) layered on (B) — start at a comfortable batch (e.g. 50) with HasMoreItems, grow by batch on LoadMore up to the ceiling. NoOpCommand/ListItem/Details/Tags/IconInfo usage is unchanged. The real hard cap to lift is Clip.Core ClipboardHistoryListCommand.MaximumLimit (NormalizeLimit clamps to 100); without raising it the page can never exceed 100 regardless of the page-level const. Keep Core work on the IListItem/array contract — do not change item shape. Settings: ClipSettingsPage is a ContentPage using Settings + ToggleSetting/ChoiceSetSetting from the toolkit; add a ChoiceSetSetting (discrete values: 50/100/250/500/Unlimited) rather than a free-text number to stay native and avoid validation. Persist via ClipSharedSettings (JSON at ClipStoragePaths.SettingsPath) which is the same file the standalone Watcher/Shell settings live in, so the standalone HistoryLimit (default 500, null=Unlimited) is readable as the ceiling.
implementationSteps: Raise the Core ceiling so the palette can exceed 100. In ClipboardHistoryListCommand.cs:10, change MaximumLimit from 100 to a value matching the standalone defaults (e.g. 1000) so NormalizeLimit (line 93, Math.Clamp(limit,1,MaximumLimit)) no longer truncates a 500-item history. Keep DefaultLimit=25 for the CLI/JSON contract (used by Parse). Add an overload boundary: optionally introduce a public const HardCeiling and clamp there, leaving MaximumLimit for the CLI. Verify QueryItemSummaries(query,limit) at ClipboardHistoryStore.cs:161 just .Take(limit)s the index — confirmed cheap, so larger limits cost only list materialization, not extra IO.

Expose the standalone HistoryLimit through ClipSharedSettings so the palette can read the user's ceiling. In ClipSharedSettings.cs: add HistoryLimit (int? — null means Unlimited, default 500) to ClipSharedSettingsSnapshot (line 18) and read it in LoadFromJson (line 50) via a new NullableIntValue helper mirroring Clip.Watcher Program.cs:894 NullableIntProperty (reads 'HistoryLimit', default 500, JSON null => null). This reuses the existing standalone-written value so palette and standalone agree on the ceiling — do not add a second key.

Add a palette-specific 'list limit' preference (the configurable cap the user picks inside Command Palette). In ClipSharedSettings.cs add PaletteListLimit (int? default null meaning 'follow HistoryLimit') to the snapshot + a SetPaletteListLimitJson/SetPaletteListLimit pair mirroring the existing Set*Json/Update pattern (lines 56-90). Storing it in the shared JSON keeps it surviving restarts and visible to both surfaces.

Create ClipPaletteListSettings.cs (new file) as a small static helper in Clip.CommandPalette that computes the effective limit: read ClipSharedSettings.Load(); ceiling = HistoryLimit is null ? int.MaxValue : HistoryLimit.Value; preferred = PaletteListLimit ?? DefaultBatch(e.g. 50); EffectiveCeiling = Min(ceiling, ClipboardHistoryListCommand.HardCeiling). Expose DefaultBatch, BatchIncrement, and a Clamp(requested) method. This isolates the limit math from page UI so it is unit-testable.

Convert ClipHistoryPage from a fixed const limit to a growable limit. In ClipHistoryPage.cs: remove 'private const int Limit = 25' (line 10); add 'private int _limit = ClipPaletteListSettings.DefaultBatch;' and 'private int _ceiling;'. In the constructor (line 18) set _ceiling = ClipPaletteListSettings.EffectiveCeiling(). In GetItems() (line 89) replace 'ClipboardHistoryListCommand.Create(store, searchText, Limit)' with '...Create(store, searchText, _limit)'.

Implement incremental load-more. In ClipHistoryPage.cs override the DynamicListPage paging members: set HasMoreItems = _limit < _ceiling whenever items are produced (after CreateItems in GetItems, before returning), and override LoadMore() to do: if(_limit>=_ceiling) return; _limit = Math.Min(_limit + ClipPaletteListSettings.BatchIncrement, _ceiling); _cachedItems = null; RaiseItemsChanged();. Because results are index-backed and capped at _ceiling, each LoadMore is a cheap re-query.

Reset paging on a new search so narrowing/expanding behaves predictably. In UpdateSearchText (line 63) when the search text actually changes, also reset _limit = ClipPaletteListSettings.DefaultBatch (in addition to the existing _cachedItems=null) so an empty query doesn't keep a giant previously-grown limit, and so a fresh search starts at one batch and can grow again. Keep the existing cache-key check (search/filter/index-stamp) intact; add _limit to the cache invalidation reasoning (a changed _limit already nulls _cachedItems in LoadMore).

Surface the configurable limit in palette settings. In ClipSettingsPage.cs constructor (after line 56) add a ChoiceSetSetting('listLimit', ...) with choices Auto(follow history limit)/50/100/250/500/Unlimited mapped to PaletteListLimit (Auto=>null, Unlimited=>a sentinel like int.MaxValue or null-with-ceiling). In OnSettingsChanged (line 63) call ClipSharedSettings.SetPaletteListLimit(parsed) and then CommandPaletteSettings.RequestExternalReload() (already used for hotkey at line 73) so an open history page rebuilds with the new cap. Add the new key const near lines 9-15.

Update the empty/error and section logic if needed: EmptyTitle (line 155) and the pinned/recent split (lines 140-150) are limit-agnostic and need no change, but confirm pinned items are not starved — since QueryRecentItemSummaries orders by recency, very old pinned items could fall outside a small batch; note that pinned items should ideally always be included. If pinned starvation is observed, fetch pinned separately (store.QueryItemSummaries with a pinned filter) and union with the recent batch before applying _limit to the recent set only. Treat this as a follow-up guard rather than core paging.

Tests/verification: add a Clip.Core test asserting NormalizeLimit no longer clamps below the new ceiling and that QueryItemSummaries returns >25 when available; manually verify in Command Palette that scrolling past the first batch triggers LoadMore and reveals older items, that the settings dropdown changes the cap and reload reflects it, and that search still narrows within the grown set. Confirm responsiveness with a 500+ item history (no UI stall on initial open because default batch stays small).
blueprints.responsivenessNotes: Keep the initial page small (DefaultBatch ~50) so first paint is instant even with a 500-1000 item history; grow only on demand via LoadMore so we never materialize the whole history up front. The existing search/filter/index-stamp cache (ClipHistoryPage lines 81-94) already prevents redundant re-queries between keystrokes — preserve it and only invalidate when _limit changes. QueryItemSummaries is index-backed and just .Take(limit)s (ClipboardHistoryStore.cs:161,208), so larger limits cost list allocation, not extra disk IO; still cap at the user's HistoryLimit ceiling so we never scan beyond what the store retains. Do all reads on the existing synchronous GetItems path (it is already fast); avoid blocking the constructor — read ClipSharedSettings once at construction and on reload, not per keystroke. Use RaiseItemsChanged() (already used) for repaint; use the toolkit HasMoreItems flag so Command Palette shows its native incremental-scroll affordance instead of a custom 'show more' row. Use a ChoiceSetSetting (discrete) not free-text to avoid input validation jank and keep it native.

blueprints.feature: Inline action coverage on preview card (only copy/pin/unpin/open/reveal, max 4)
blueprints.sdkNotes: ContentPage exposes both Commands and MoreCommands (IContextItem[]); ClipItemPreviewPage already populates Commands via item.Actions.Select(CreateContextCommand) (ClipItemPreviewPage.cs:21), so rename/edit-text/delete/save-as-file/copy-path ARE already reachable from the preview page's command bar / Ctrl+K menu today — the gap is ONLY the inline Adaptive Card buttons and a missing Paste verb, not full unreachability. Adaptive Card actions are emitted as Action.Submit blocks in BuildActions and dispatched through FormContent.SubmitForm(payload)->ActionIdFrom (ClipItemPreviewCard.cs:65-82,208-225). CRITICAL constraint: SubmitForm currently routes EVERY action through ClipHistoryActionCommand only. rename/edit-text/save-as-file require navigating to a ClipItemTextFormPage (a ContentPage), and copy-path needs ClipCopyPathCommand; FormContent.SubmitForm returns an ICommandResult and CANNOT push a new page (it can only Dismiss/KeepOpen/GoBack/ShowToast/GoToPage). Therefore form-style verbs (rename/edit-text/save-as-file) must NOT be made inline Submit buttons routed through ClipHistoryActionCommand — either keep them as page Commands only, or emit them as Action.Submit that returns CommandResult.GoToPage(formPage) from an extended SubmitForm. Inline-button-safe verbs that the existing in-process executor (ClipboardHistoryActionExecutor.Execute / ClipHistoryActionCommand.TryInvokeInProcess, ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:64-134) already handles end-to-end: copy, pin, unpin, move-pin-up, move-pin-down, delete, open, reveal, and copy-path (copy-path needs a small SubmitForm branch since it is not handled by ClipHistoryActionCommand today). 'paste' does not exist anywhere in Core's ClipboardHistoryListAction.ForHistoryItem (ClipboardHistoryListCommand.cs:171-281) nor in the executor — it must be added as a new verb if desired (Clip.App standalone uses focus-restore + SendInput Ctrl+V which Command Palette cannot do reliably; Command Palette's pragmatic 'paste' == copy-to-clipboard then CommandResult.Dismiss so the palette closes and the user pastes). Destructive 'delete' should be styled with Adaptive Card "style":"destructive" on its Action.Submit. Default/Enter mapping on a ContentPage is governed by the IListItem.Command that opened the page and the page's first Command; to make Enter perform the default action, order Commands so the default (copy, or paste) is first, and mark the matching inline button as the card's primary action.
implementationSteps: Decide the inline-button policy: inline Adaptive Card buttons cover only verbs the card's SubmitForm can fully execute in-process without page navigation = copy, paste(new), pin, unpin, move-pin-up, move-pin-down, delete, open, reveal, copy-path. Form verbs (rename, edit-text, save-as-file) stay reachable as ClipItemPreviewPage.Commands/MoreCommands (already wired at ClipItemPreviewPage.cs:21,38-54) and are NOT duplicated as inline buttons unless you implement GoToPage routing (optional step 7).

In Clip.Core ClipboardHistoryListCommand.cs ForHistoryItem (line 171), optionally add a 'paste' action FIRST when CanCopy(item) is true: new ClipboardHistoryListAction(Id:"paste", Label:"Paste", Executable:"Clip.Watcher.exe", Arguments:["copy", item.Id], RequiresFullItem:true). Set DefaultActionId to 'paste' when present (ClipboardHistoryListItem.FromHistoryItem line 156 currently uses actions.First()). If you do NOT want a separate Core verb, skip this and treat the existing 'copy' as the default instead.

In ClipItemPreviewCard.BuildActions (ClipItemPreviewCard.cs:208-225) replace the whitelist set {copy,pin,unpin,open,reveal} with the full inline-safe set {paste,copy,pin,unpin,move-pin-up,move-pin-down,delete,save-as-file?,copy-path,open,reveal} and REMOVE the .Take(4) cap (or raise it to .Take(8)). Preserve Core's action ordering so the most-used verbs render first.

In BuildActions, emit a destructive style for delete: when action.Id=="delete" add "\"style\": \"destructive\"" to the Action.Submit JSON so it is visually distinct per the gap's requirement. Keep the existing JsonString escaping helper.

Extend ClipItemPreviewCard.SubmitForm (ClipItemPreviewCard.cs:65-82) so copy-path is handled: before constructing ClipHistoryActionCommand, branch on actionId=="copy-path" -> return new ClipCopyPathCommand(_listItem.FilePaths).Invoke(). All other inline IDs already flow correctly through ClipHistoryActionCommand because TryInvokeInProcess/ClipboardHistoryActionExecutor handle pin/unpin/move/delete/copy and open/reveal (ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:64-134). For paste, ClipHistoryActionCommand sees Arguments[0]=="copy" and will copy+Dismiss, which is the desired paste behavior.

Map Enter to the default action on the preview PAGE: in ClipItemPreviewPage constructor (ClipItemPreviewPage.cs:21) order Commands so the action whose Id == item.DefaultActionId (paste, else copy) is first; the first CommandContextItem becomes the page's default invoked on Enter. Optionally also set the page-opening ListItem.Command in ClipHistoryPage.CreateListItem to that default so Enter on the list row and Enter on the preview page agree.

OPTIONAL (full inline parity incl. form verbs): change ClipItemPreviewCard's afterHistoryMutation wiring to also receive a navigation callback, and in SubmitForm return CommandResult.GoToPage(...) with ClipItemTextFormPage.Rename/EditText/SaveAsFile for those IDs instead of excluding them. This lets rename/edit-text/save-as-file be inline buttons too. Confirm GoToPage is supported by the installed SDK build before relying on it; otherwise leave these as page Commands only.

De-duplicate the now-identical CreateContextCommand + IconForAction logic shared by ClipHistoryPage.cs:215-231 and ClipItemPreviewPage.cs:38-81 into one internal static helper (e.g. ClipHistoryCommandFactory) so the preview page and list row build identical action sets and icons; this prevents future drift between the two surfaces. Low risk, optional within this gap.

Verify each ClipboardItemKind (Text, Link, Image, Files, Color) renders only its valid inline buttons because Core's ForHistoryItem already gates copy/open/reveal/save-as-file/copy-path by kind; the widened whitelist just surfaces whatever Core produced, so no per-kind filtering is needed in the card.

Build Clip.CommandPalette (net9.0-windows) and Clip.Core; manually exercise: open preview for a text item (paste/copy/pin/delete inline, delete red), a files item (copy-path/open/reveal inline), an image item, a pinned item (unpin/move-up/move-down inline). Confirm Enter triggers paste/copy and palette dismisses, and that rename/edit-text/save-as-file still work from the command bar.
blueprints.responsivenessNotes: All inline verbs route through the existing in-process executor (ClipboardHistoryActionExecutor.Execute / ClipHistoryActionCommand.TryInvokeInProcess), so no extra process spawn for pin/unpin/move/delete/copy/paste — they mutate the SQLite store directly and call _afterHistoryMutation to refresh the list, keeping the UI snappy. open/reveal still Process.Start a launch but only on demand. Card stays declarative JSON built synchronously from already-loaded _fullItem/_listItem (no I/O in BuildActions), so widening the whitelist adds zero latency. Keep using IconInfo segoe-glyph codes already defined in IconForAction for native CommandBar parity. Mark delete with Adaptive Card destructive style rather than a custom color so it follows the host theme. Do not push pages from SubmitForm unless the SDK build confirms GoToPage support; falling back to page Commands keeps behavior reliable. Removing .Take(4) is safe because Core already kind-gates the action list, so the button count stays bounded (typically 4-7).

blueprints.feature: Tray: paste-latest / quick paste of most recent item without opening UI
blueprints.sdkNotes: Use a top-level CommandItem backed by an InvokableCommand (Microsoft.CommandPalette.Extensions.Toolkit.InvokableCommand), the same base class already used by ClipShellActionCommand and ClipHistoryActionCommand. Return CommandResult.Dismiss() on success so Command Palette closes and foreground focus returns to the user's prior app BEFORE the Ctrl+V fires (the actual paste runs in the separate Clip.Watcher.exe process). Return CommandResult.KeepOpen() with a ToastStatusMessage(StatusMessage{State=MessageState.Error}) on failure (e.g. empty history / watcher not found) — identical error pattern to ClipShellActionCommand.ShowError (ClipShellActionCommand.cs:64) and ClipHistoryActionCommand.ShowError (ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:136). Surface the command in ClipCommandsProvider.TopLevelCommands() as a second CommandItem (provider already returns ICommandItem[]; ClipCommandsProvider.cs:26) so it is keyword-searchable from the CmdPal root, AND add it to ClipHistoryPage.CreateContextCommands() (ClipHistoryPage.cs:29) as a CommandContextItem so it is reachable from the history page's More-commands flyout. Set IconInfo("") (paste glyph) for visual parity with the existing Segoe Fluent icon set used throughout (e.g. ClipHistoryActionCommand.IconForAction). Do NOT attempt SendKeys/Clipboard.SetContent inside the palette process — the CmdPal extension host cannot reliably restore foreground focus to the target app, which is exactly why the tray path lives in the watcher; delegate instead.
implementationSteps: Confirm the delegation contract: Clip.Watcher.exe already exposes a CLI verb 'paste <id>' (Clip.Watcher/Program.cs:184-185 -> SetClipboard(id, paste:true) -> TrySetClipboardItem at Program.cs:253, which calls Clipboard.SetText/SetImage/SetFileDropList then SendKeys.SendWait("^v") at Program.cs:279). This is the same mechanism PasteLatestFromTray (Program.cs:1418) uses via TrySetClipboardItem. No watcher changes are required — the palette can reach paste-latest entirely through this existing verb.

Create ClipPasteLatestCommand.cs: a sealed partial InvokableCommand. Constructor sets Name="Paste latest item", Icon=new IconInfo(""). Hold a Lazy<ClipboardHistoryStore> OR accept the shared Lazy<ClipboardHistoryStore> from ClipHistoryPage so the in-memory summary cache is reused (see _store at ClipHistoryPage.cs:11 = ClipboardHistoryStore.OpenForCommandSurface()). Preferred: accept the existing Lazy<ClipboardHistoryStore> via constructor to avoid opening a second store handle.

In Invoke(): call store.QueryRecentItemSummaries(1) (ClipboardHistoryStore.cs:183) and take FirstOrDefault. If null, ShowError("No clipboard items yet.") and return CommandResult.KeepOpen() — mirrors the tray's empty-history balloon at Program.cs:1423.

Resolve the watcher exe: var exe = ClipExecutableLocator.Resolve("Clip.Watcher.exe") (ClipExecutableLocator.cs:13). The locator already searches AppContext.BaseDirectory + install roots and is the same call other palette commands use. If null, ShowError("Clip.Watcher.exe was not found.") and return KeepOpen().

Launch the paste: build ProcessStartInfo { FileName=exe, UseShellExecute=false, WorkingDirectory=Path.GetDirectoryName(exe) }, then startInfo.ArgumentList.Add("paste"); startInfo.ArgumentList.Add(item.Id); Process.Start(startInfo); — same launch shape as ClipHistoryActionCommand.Invoke (ClipHistoryActionCommand.cs:42-54) and ClipShellActionCommand.Invoke (ClipShellActionCommand.cs:37-54). Wrap in try/catch -> ShowError(ex.Message)+KeepOpen on failure.

Return CommandResult.Dismiss() immediately after Process.Start so CmdPal closes and the OS restores focus to the previously-active window; the detached watcher process then sets the clipboard and sends Ctrl+V into that window. (The watcher's SendKeys ^v at Program.cs:279 lands in whatever window now holds focus, which is the user's app once the palette has dismissed.)
Add a ShowError helper to the new class identical to ClipShellActionCommand.ShowError (ToastStatusMessage with MessageState.Error).

Wire the top-level entry: in ClipCommandsProvider.TopLevelCommands() (ClipCommandsProvider.cs:26) construct the historyPage first, then add a second CommandItem(new ClipPasteLatestCommand(historyPage.Store)) { Title="Paste latest item", Subtitle="Paste the most recent clipboard item" } to the _commands array. (Expose the page's Lazy<ClipboardHistoryStore> via an internal property on ClipHistoryPage so the command shares it.)

Wire the page-level entry: add new CommandContextItem(new ClipPasteLatestCommand(_store)) { Title="Paste latest item", Icon=new IconInfo("") } to ClipHistoryPage.CreateContextCommands() (ClipHistoryPage.cs:29) so it appears in the More-commands flyout alongside Settings/Open Clip.exe.

Add an internal accessor on ClipHistoryPage (e.g. internal Lazy<ClipboardHistoryStore> Store => _store;) so both the provider and the context item construct the command against the one shared store instance.

Build the Clip.CommandPalette project (dotnet build) and smoke-test: copy an item, open CmdPal, type 'Paste latest', invoke, confirm the palette dismisses and Ctrl+V lands in the foreground app; then test empty-history and missing-watcher error toasts.
blueprints.responsivenessNotes: Invoke() must stay non-blocking: QueryRecentItemSummaries(1) is a cheap top-index/cache read (ClipboardHistoryStore.cs:192 returns from _summaryItemsCache when warm; otherwise reads only the top summary index, not full items), and Process.Start is fire-and-forget — do NOT WaitForExit. The actual clipboard write + SendKeys runs in the watcher process, keeping the palette UI thread free. Reuse the page's existing Lazy<ClipboardHistoryStore> (ClipHistoryPage.cs:11) rather than opening a new store so no extra file handles or index loads occur on first invoke.
